name: Barbell Back Squat
instructions: Unrack the barbell so it rests securely across your upper traps or rear delts.
Step back and set your feet roughly shoulder-width apart, toes pointed slightly outward.
Brace your core, take a deep breath, and initiate the movement by pushing your hips back and bending your knees.
Lower yourself until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor.
Drive through your mid-foot to stand back up to the starting position.
tips: Keep your chest up and a neutral spine throughout the movement. Do not let your knees cave inward as you push up.

name: Standard Push-Up
instructions: Start in a high plank position with your hands placed slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Keep your body in a straight line from your head to your heels.
Lower your body by bending your elbows until your chest is just above the floor.
Push back up to the starting position by fully extending your arms.
tips: Keep your elbows tucked at a 45-degree angle to your body to protect your shoulders. Do not let your lower back sag.

name: Conventional Deadlift
instructions: Stand with your mid-foot under the barbell, feet hip-width apart.
Hinge at your hips and grip the bar just outside your knees.
Drop your hips slightly, lift your chest, and flatten your back.
Take a deep breath, brace your core, and drive through the floor with your legs to lift the weight.
Stand tall at the top with your hips fully extended, then hinge at the hips to return the bar to the floor.
tips: Keep the barbell close to your body (dragging up your shins). Avoid rounding your lower back at any point during the lift.

name: Pull-Up
instructions: Grab the pull-up bar with an overhand grip, hands slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Hang freely with your arms fully extended (dead hang).
Pull your shoulder blades down and back, then pull your chest up to the bar.
Pause briefly at the top, then lower yourself slowly and with control back to the starting position.
tips: Focus on driving your elbows down to the floor rather than just pulling with your biceps. Minimize swinging (kipping) to maximize muscle engagement.

name: Dumbbell Overhead Press (Seated)
instructions: Sit on a bench with back support, holding a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
Kick the dumbbells up to shoulder height, palms facing forward.
Press the dumbbells overhead until your arms are fully extended.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at your shoulders.
tips: Keep your core tight and avoid excessively arching your lower back. Don't let the dumbbells clank together at the top.

name: Forearm Plank
instructions: Place your forearms on the floor, elbows aligned directly under your shoulders.
Extend your legs straight out behind you, resting on your toes.
Squeeze your glutes and brace your core to create a straight, rigid line from your head to your heels.
Hold this position for the desired amount of time.
tips: Don't let your hips sag toward the floor or pike up toward the ceiling. Keep your neck in a neutral position by looking at the floor in front of your hands.

name: Romanian Deadlift (RDL)
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, holding the barbell or dumbbells with an overhand grip in front of your thighs.
Keep a soft bend in your knees (do not lock them out).
Hinge at your hips, pushing your glutes backward while keeping your back perfectly flat.
Lower the weight down the front of your legs until you feel a deep stretch in your hamstrings.
Drive your hips forward to return to the starting, upright position.
tips: The movement should come entirely from your hips moving backward and forward, not from bending your knees or rounding your spine.

name: Walking Lunges
instructions: Stand tall with your feet together.
Take a large step forward with your right leg.
Lower your hips until both knees are bent at approximately a 90-degree angle (your back knee should lightly tap or hover just above the ground).
Push off your right foot, bring your left foot forward, and step directly into a lunge on the left side.
tips: Keep your torso upright. Ensure your front knee tracks over your toes and does not collapse inward.

name: Dumbbell Bicep Curl
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand with your arms fully extended and palms facing forward (supinated grip).
Keeping your elbows tucked close to your torso, curl the weights up toward your shoulders.
Squeeze your biceps hard at the top of the movement.
Lower the dumbbells slowly and with control back to the starting position.
tips: Do not use momentum by swinging your torso. Keep your elbows stationary; they shouldn't move forward as you curl the weight.

name: Kettlebell Swing
instructions: Stand with feet slightly wider than shoulder-width, with the kettlebell on the floor slightly in front of you.
Hinge at your hips to grab the kettlebell handle with both hands.
Hike the kettlebell back between your legs.
Explosively drive your hips forward, squeezing your glutes, which will propel the kettlebell up to about chest height.
Let the kettlebell swing back down between your legs and immediately transition into the next rep.
tips: This is a hip hinge, not a squat. The power comes entirely from your glutes and hips, not your arms (your arms just guide the weight).

name: Barbell Bench Press
instructions: Lie flat on the bench with your eyes directly under the bar.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Unrack the bar and hold it straight over your chest with arms locked.
Lower the bar slowly to your mid-chest, keeping your elbows at about a 45-degree angle to your torso.
Press the bar back up to the starting position.
tips: Plant your feet firmly on the ground and maintain a slight natural arch in your lower back. Don't bounce the bar off your chest.

name: Bent-Over Barbell Row
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a barbell with an overhand grip.
Hinge at your hips until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor, keeping a slight bend in your knees and a flat back.
Let the bar hang directly in front of you with arms fully extended.
Pull the bar towards your lower ribcage, squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Lower the bar back to the starting position with control.
tips: Avoid using momentum by jerking your torso up. Keep your neck neutral and your core braced to protect your lower back.

name: Leg Press
instructions: Sit on the leg press machine with your back and head resting comfortably against the pad.
Place your feet on the sled roughly shoulder-width apart.
Disengage the safety catches and press the sled up until your legs are extended (but not locked out).
Lower the sled slowly by bending your knees until they form a 90-degree angle.
Push through your heels to return the sled to the starting position.
tips: Never lock your knees out completely at the top of the movement. Keep your lower back pressed firmly against the pad.

name: Parallel Bar Dips
instructions: Grip the parallel bars and jump or step up so your arms are fully extended and supporting your body weight.
Lean your torso slightly forward.
Lower your body by bending your elbows until your shoulders are below your elbows.
Push yourself back up to the starting position by extending your arms.
tips: Leaning forward targets the chest more, while staying upright targets the triceps. Do not flare your elbows out too wide.

name: Lat Pulldown
instructions: Sit down at the machine and adjust the knee pad so your legs are locked in firmly.
Grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width with an overhand grip.
Lean back slightly and pull the bar down smoothly to your upper chest.
Squeeze your lats at the bottom, then slowly return the bar to the starting position, letting your arms fully extend.
tips: Pull with your elbows, driving them down towards the floor. Do not swing your torso backward to pull the weight.

name: Seated Cable Row
instructions: Sit at the cable row station with your feet on the platforms and knees slightly bent.
Grab the V-grip handle, sit upright with a tall chest, and arms fully extended.
Pull the handle towards your belly button, squeezing your shoulder blades together at the peak of the movement.
Slowly extend your arms to return to the starting position without letting your lower back round.
tips: Keep your torso stationary; do not rock back and forth. Focus on retracting your scapula (pinching your shoulder blades).

name: Standing Calf Raises
instructions: Stand on the edge of the platform of the machine with your heels hanging off.
Position the shoulder pads comfortably over your shoulders.
Lower your heels as far down as possible to get a deep stretch in your calves.
Push up onto your toes as high as you can, contracting your calves forcefully.
Hold the top position for a second before lowering back down.
tips: Use a full range of motion. Do not bounce at the bottom of the movement.

name: Triceps Rope Pushdown
instructions: Attach a rope to a high pulley on a cable machine.
Grip the rope with both hands, palms facing each other, and stand with feet shoulder-width apart.
Keep your elbows tucked into your sides and your torso upright.
Push the rope down until your arms are fully extended, spreading the ends of the rope apart at the bottom.
Slowly return to the starting position.
tips: Your elbows should act as a hinge and remain locked in place. Do not let them drift forward as the weight goes up.

name: Dumbbell Lateral Raise
instructions: Stand tall holding a dumbbell in each hand by your sides, palms facing inward.
Maintain a slight bend in your elbows.
Raise the dumbbells straight out to your sides until your arms are parallel to the floor.
Pause briefly at the top, then slowly lower the weights back to your sides.
tips: Imagine pouring water out of a pitcher at the top of the movement to fully engage the lateral deltoid. Do not shrug your shoulders.

name: Bulgarian Split Squat
instructions: Stand a few feet in front of a bench, holding dumbbells by your sides.
Reach one foot back and place the top of your foot on the bench.
Lower your hips until your front thigh is parallel to the floor, ensuring your front knee doesn't pass your toes.
Drive through the heel of your front foot to stand back up.
tips: Leaning your torso slightly forward will target the glutes more, while staying upright will target the quads. Keep your core tight for balance.

name: Leg Extension
instructions: Sit on the machine and adjust the pad so it sits comfortably on your lower shins, just above your ankles.
Adjust the backrest so your knees line up with the pivot point of the machine.
Grip the handles by your sides and extend your legs until they are straight.
Squeeze your quads at the top, then slowly lower the weight back down.
tips: Do not use momentum to kick the weight up. Control the eccentric (lowering) phase.

name: Lying Leg Curl
instructions: Lie face down on the machine, adjusting the roller pad to sit just above your heels.
Ensure your knees line up with the machine's pivot point.
Curl your legs up as far as possible, squeezing your hamstrings at the top.
Slowly lower the weight back to the starting position.
tips: Keep your hips pressed firmly into the bench; do not let your lower back arch to lift the weight.

name: Russian Twist
instructions: Sit on the floor with your knees bent and feet lifted slightly off the ground (or planted for an easier variation).
Lean back slightly to form a V-shape with your torso and thighs.
Clasp your hands together or hold a weight in front of your chest.
Twist your torso to the right, touching the weight or your hands to the floor beside your hip.
Twist back to the center and then to the left.
tips: Rotate your entire torso, not just your arms. Keep your neck neutral and your core braced.

name: Cable Face Pull
instructions: Set a cable pulley to upper-chest or face height and attach a rope.
Grab the rope with an overhand grip, thumbs pointing backward.
Step back to create tension, stand tall, and brace your core.
Pull the rope towards your face, splitting the handles apart so they pass on either side of your head.
Squeeze your upper back muscles, then slowly return to the starting position.
tips: Focus on pulling your shoulder blades together rather than just pulling with your arms. Keep your elbows high.

name: Barbell Front Squat
instructions: Rest the barbell across your front delts and clavicles.
Cross your arms to hold the bar in place, or use a clean grip with just your fingertips under the bar and elbows pointed high.
Step back, feet shoulder-width apart.
Keep your chest up and elbows high as you lower your hips into a squat.
Drive through your feet to stand back up to the starting position.
tips: Keeping your elbows pointing forward and up is crucial to prevent the bar from rolling off your shoulders. This exercise requires a very upright torso.

name: Incline Dumbbell Bench Press
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45 degree incline.
Sit on the bench with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
Kick the weights up to shoulder level and lie back.
Press the dumbbells straight up above your upper chest until your arms are fully extended.
Lower the weights slowly until they are in line with your upper chest.
tips: Keep your feet flat on the floor and your shoulder blades retracted. Don't let the dumbbells clank at the top.

name: Barbell Hip Thrust
instructions: Sit on the ground with your upper back resting against a bench.
Roll a padded barbell over your hips.
Plant your feet flat on the floor, roughly shoulder-width apart, with your knees bent.
Drive through your heels and squeeze your glutes to lift your hips toward the ceiling until your thighs and torso are parallel to the floor.
Lower your hips back down to the ground with control.
tips: Ensure your shins are vertical at the top of the movement. Keep your chin tucked and avoid overextending your lower back.

name: Hammer Curls
instructions: Stand tall holding a dumbbell in each hand by your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing each other).
Keep your elbows locked at your sides.
Curl the dumbbells up towards your shoulders while maintaining the neutral grip.
Squeeze your forearms and biceps at the top, then slowly lower the weights.
tips: Do not sway your back to lift the weight. The neutral grip heavily targets the forearms and the muscle beneath the bicep.

name: Arnold Press
instructions: Sit on a bench with back support, holding a dumbbell in each hand in front of your face with palms facing towards you (supinated grip).
As you press the dumbbells overhead, rotate your wrists outward so that your palms face forward at the top of the movement.
Reverse the motion as you lower the weights, bringing them back in front of your face with palms facing you.
tips: Use a smooth, continuous motion. This exercise offers a larger range of motion than a standard shoulder press.

name: Mountain Climbers
instructions: Start in a high plank position with your hands shoulder-width apart.
Keep your core tight and your back flat.
Drive your right knee up towards your chest.
Quickly switch legs, driving your left knee towards your chest while extending your right leg back.
Continue alternating legs at a fast pace.
tips: Keep your hips down; do not let them bounce up and down into the air. Keep your weight distributed over your hands.

name: Seated Leg Curl
instructions: Sit on the machine with your back against the pad and adjust the lap pad to secure your thighs.
Place your lower legs on top of the padded lever, just below the calves.
Pull the lever down and back towards your thighs by flexing your knees.
Squeeze your hamstrings at the bottom of the movement.
Slowly return to the starting position.
tips: Control the weight on the way up; don't let it just snap back to the top. Keep your toes pointed straight or slightly flexed.

name: Hack Squat
instructions: Position your back and shoulders flat against the pads of the hack squat machine.
Place your feet on the platform roughly shoulder-width apart.
Disengage the safety handles and lower the sled by bending your knees until your thighs are at least parallel to the platform.
Drive through your heels to push the sled back up to the starting position.
tips: This machine removes the balance requirement of a free-weight squat, allowing you to isolate and overload the quads safely. Do not let your knees cave inward.

name: Cable Crossover
instructions: Set the pulleys on a dual cable machine to the high position and grab a handle in each hand.
Step forward slightly to create tension on the cables, keeping one foot slightly in front of the other for stability.
Maintain a slight bend in your elbows and a proud chest.
Bring the handles down and together in front of your torso in a wide, hugging motion.
Squeeze your chest at the bottom, then slowly let your arms open back up.
tips: Imagine wrapping your arms around a large tree. Keep your elbows fixed in slightly bent position; do not turn this into a pressing motion.

name: Skullcrushers (Lying Triceps Extension)
instructions: Lie flat on a bench holding an EZ-curl bar with a narrow, overhand grip.
Press the bar straight up over your chest.
Keeping your upper arms perfectly stationary, bend your elbows to lower the bar towards your forehead (or just behind your head).
Extend your elbows to press the bar back up to the starting position.
tips: Your elbows should point toward the ceiling throughout the movement. Flare them outward as little as possible.

name: Seated Calf Raise
instructions: Sit on the machine and place your toes on the lower platform with your heels hanging off.
Position the padded lever over your lower thighs, just above the knees.
Lift the weight slightly and disengage the safety catch.
Lower your heels as far as possible to stretch the calves.
Drive through your toes to raise the weight as high as you can, flexing your calves.
tips: Because your knees are bent, this exercise primarily targets the soleus (the wider muscle underneath the gastrocnemius). Use a slow, controlled tempo.

name: Close-Grip Lat Pulldown
instructions: Attach a V-grip handle to the lat pulldown machine and sit with your thighs secured under the pads.
Grab the handle with both hands, palms facing each other.
Lean back slightly, keep your chest up, and pull the handle down to your upper chest.
Squeeze your back muscles, then slowly let the weight back up until your arms are fully extended.
tips: This variation allows for a deeper stretch in the lats and incorporates more bicep activation than a wide-grip pulldown.

name: Dumbbell Shrugs
instructions: Stand tall holding a dumbbell in each hand by your sides, arms fully extended.
Keeping your arms straight, elevate your shoulders straight up towards your ears (shrugging).
Hold the top contraction for a second.
Slowly lower your shoulders back to the starting, relaxed position.
tips: Move straight up and down. Do not roll your shoulders in a circular motion, as this can cause rotator cuff strain.

name: Ab Wheel Rollout
instructions: Kneel on a mat and grasp the ab wheel handles with both hands.
Position the wheel on the floor directly in front of your knees.
Brace your core tightly and slowly roll the wheel straight forward, extending your body as far as you can without letting your lower back sag.
Use your core to pull yourself back to the starting kneeling position.
tips: Your hips and shoulders should move together. Do not simply push your arms out while leaving your hips behind. If your lower back hurts, you are rolling out too far.

name: Decline Barbell Bench Press
instructions: Secure your feet at the end of the decline bench and lie back.
Grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width and unrack it.
Lower the bar slowly to your lower chest.
Press the bar back up in a straight line until your arms are fully extended.
tips: Having a spotter is highly recommended for this exercise since the bar path is directly over your neck and face. Keep your shoulder blades pinned back.

name: Good Mornings
instructions: Unrack the barbell exactly as you would for a back squat, resting it across your upper back.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and a slight bend in your knees.
Keeping your back perfectly flat and your core braced, hinge at your hips to push your glutes backward.
Lower your torso until it is nearly parallel to the floor (or until you feel a deep stretch in your hamstrings).
Drive your hips forward to stand back up.
tips: Start with very light weight. The movement is identical to a Romanian Deadlift, just with the weight on your back instead of in your hands.

name: Flat Dumbbell Flyes
instructions: Lie flat on a bench holding two dumbbells straight up over your chest with your palms facing each other.
Keep a slight bend in your elbows.
Slowly lower the dumbbells out to your sides in a wide arc until you feel a stretch in your chest.
Squeeze your chest muscles to bring the dumbbells back up along the same arc to the starting position.
tips: Do not go too deep, as this puts excessive strain on the shoulder joint. The motion should feel like hugging a large barrel.

name: T-Bar Row
equipment: T-Bar Row Machine (or Barbell with Landmine Attachment)
instructions: Stand over the bar with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Hinge at the hips, keeping your back flat, and grasp the handles.
Pull the weight up towards your chest, keeping your elbows close to your body.
Squeeze your back at the top, then slowly lower the weight back down without letting the plates touch the floor.
tips: Avoid using your lower back to jerk the weight up. Keep your core heavily braced to support your spine.

name: Preacher Curl
instructions: Sit at a preacher bench and adjust the seat so your armpits rest comfortably over the top of the pad.
Grasp an EZ-curl bar with an underhand grip, shoulder-width apart.
Rest your triceps flat against the pad.
Curl the bar up towards your shoulders, squeezing your biceps hard.
Lower the bar slowly until your arms are fully extended.
tips: The pad prevents you from using momentum, making this a strict isolation exercise. Do not hyperextend your elbows at the bottom.

name: Machine Rear Delt Fly (Reverse Pec Deck)
instructions: Adjust the machine handles to the rearmost position and sit facing the pad.
Grasp the horizontal or vertical handles.
Keeping your arms relatively straight with a slight bend in the elbow, pull the handles back in an arc.
Squeeze your rear delts and upper back together at the end of the movement.
Return the handles to the starting position with control.
tips: Focus on using the backs of your shoulders to move the weight, not just squeezing your shoulder blades together.

name: Step-Ups
instructions: Stand facing a box or bench.
Place your right foot firmly on the top of the box.
Drive through your right foot to step up, bringing your left foot up to rest beside your right.
Carefully step back down with the left foot first, followed by the right.
Alternate the leading leg on each rep.
tips: Try to minimize the push-off from your trailing leg; make the working (top) leg do the majority of the lifting.

name: Hyperextension (Back Extension)
instructions: Position yourself on the bench with your thighs resting firmly on the pads and your ankles secured under the rollers.
Adjust the upper pad so you can bend fully at the waist without restriction.
Cross your arms over your chest.
Lower your upper body slowly until you feel a slight stretch in your hamstrings.
Raise your torso back up until your body forms a straight line.
tips: Do not hyperextend your back past a straight line at the top, as this can compress the spine.

name: Dumbbell Pullover
instructions: Lie perpendicular across a flat bench, with only your upper back and shoulders resting on the pad. Plant your feet flat on the floor, hips bridged up.
Hold a single dumbbell with both hands straight up over your chest.
Keep a slight bend in your elbows and lower the weight in an arc behind your head until you feel a stretch in your lats and chest.
Pull the dumbbell back up over your chest in the same arc.
tips: Keep your core tight and your hips low to maximize the stretch. Do not bend your elbows into a triceps extension.

name: Triceps Kickbacks
instructions: Place your left knee and left hand on a flat bench for support. Your back should be flat and nearly parallel to the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in your right hand and bring your upper arm parallel to your torso, elbow bent at 90 degrees.
Keeping your upper arm stationary, extend your elbow to kick the weight back until your arm is straight.
Squeeze your triceps at the top, then slowly lower the dumbbell back to the 90-degree angle.
tips: Do not swing the weight. If you have to use momentum from your shoulder, the weight is too heavy.

name: Glute Bridge
instructions: Lie on your back with your knees bent and feet flat on the floor, hip-width apart.
Place your arms at your sides with palms facing down.
Drive through your heels and squeeze your glutes to lift your hips toward the ceiling until your body forms a straight line from your knees to your shoulders.
Hold for a second at the top, then slowly lower your hips back down.
tips: Avoid over-arching your lower back at the top; the movement should come entirely from your glutes.

name: V-Ups
instructions: Lie flat on your back with your legs straight and arms extended over your head.
In one explosive movement, lift your legs and your torso simultaneously, reaching your hands toward your toes to form a 'V' shape.
Balance on your sit bones for a split second.
Slowly lower your arms and legs back down to the floor.
tips: Keep your legs as straight as possible. If this is too difficult, you can modify it by bringing your knees into your chest instead of keeping your legs straight.

name: Machine Hip Abduction
instructions: Sit on the machine and place your legs inside the knee pads.
Adjust the starting position so your knees are close together.
Push your knees outward against the pads as far as comfortable, squeezing your outer glutes.
Slowly return to the starting position.
tips: Sit up tall and keep your core engaged. Avoid letting the weight stack slam down between reps.

name: Machine Hip Adduction
instructions: Sit on the machine and place your legs outside the knee pads.
Adjust the machine so your legs are spread as wide as comfortably possible.
Squeeze your thighs together to push the pads inward until they touch.
Slowly let the pads return to the wide starting position.
tips: Control the eccentric (opening) portion of the movement to avoid groin strains. Keep your back flat against the pad.

name: Dumbbell Reverse Lunge
instructions: Stand tall holding a dumbbell in each hand by your sides.
Take a large step backward with your right foot.
Lower your hips so that your left thigh becomes parallel to the floor, with your right knee hovering just above the ground.
Drive through the heel of your left (front) foot to stand back up to the starting position.
Alternate legs or complete all reps on one side before switching.
tips: Reverse lunges generally put less stress on the knee joint than forward lunges. Keep your chest up.

name: Cable Crunch
instructions: Attach a rope to a high pulley on a cable station.
Kneel a few feet away, facing the machine, and grab the rope with both hands.
Pull the rope down so your hands are positioned near your ears or forehead.
With your hips locked in place, flex your spine forward, bringing your elbows toward your knees.
Squeeze your abs hard, then slowly return to the upright kneeling position.
tips: Your hips should remain stationary; the movement is a flexion of the spine, not a hip hinge. Don't pull with your arms.

name: Dumbbell Sumo Squat
instructions: Stand with your feet significantly wider than shoulder-width apart, toes pointed outward at a 45-degree angle.
Hold a single heavy dumbbell or kettlebell vertically in front of your waist with both hands.
Keeping your chest up and back straight, bend your knees to lower your hips.
Lower until your thighs are parallel to the floor, ensuring your knees track over your toes.
Drive through your heels to stand back up.
tips: The wide stance and toe flare heavily recruit the inner thighs (adductors) alongside the glutes and quads.

name: Barbell Push Press
instructions: Stand holding a barbell across your front shoulders (clavicles) with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Perform a quick, shallow dip by bending your knees slightly (do not hinge at the hips).
Explosively drive your legs straight to generate upward momentum.
As the bar launches off your shoulders, press it overhead until your arms are fully locked out.
Lower the bar back to your shoulders with control.
tips: This is a power movement. The legs should do a significant portion of the work to get the bar moving. Keep your core heavily braced.

name: Concentration Curls
instructions: Sit on the edge of a bench with your legs spread wide.
Hold a dumbbell in your right hand and rest the back of your right upper arm against the inside of your right thigh.
Let the weight hang straight down with your arm fully extended.
Curl the dumbbell up toward your shoulder, isolating the bicep.
Pause and squeeze at the top, then slowly lower it back down.
tips: Do not lean back or use momentum to swing the weight up. The thigh acts as an anchor to strictly isolate the bicep.

name: Reverse Barbell Curl
instructions: Stand tall holding a barbell with an overhand (pronated) grip, hands shoulder-width apart.
Keep your elbows tucked into your sides.
Curl the bar upward toward your shoulders, focusing on the forearms and outer biceps.
Slowly lower the bar back to the starting position.
tips: You will need to use less weight than a standard bicep curl. Keep your wrists straight and rigid throughout the movement.

name: Overhead Cable Triceps Extension
instructions: Set a cable pulley to a low or medium height and attach a rope.
Face away from the machine, grasp the rope overhead, and step forward to create tension.
Stand in a split stance for balance, with your elbows bent and pointing up toward the ceiling.
Extend your arms, pressing the rope forward and up until your elbows are locked out.
Slowly let your hands return to the starting position behind your head.
tips: Getting the arms overhead fully stretches the long head of the triceps. Keep your upper arms stationary.

name: Pendlay Row
instructions: Stand over a loaded barbell with a slightly wider than shoulder-width stance.
Hinge at the hips so your torso is exactly parallel to the floor. Your knees should be slightly bent.
Grab the bar with an overhand grip.
Explosively pull the bar directly to your lower chest/upper stomach.
Return the bar entirely to the floor. The weight must start from a dead stop on the ground for every single rep.
tips: Do not let your torso rise up during the pull; it must stay parallel to the floor. This builds explosive back power.

name: Sumo Deadlift
instructions: Stand with an ultra-wide stance, toes pointed outward, with the barbell over your mid-foot.
Drop your hips and grip the bar with your hands placed *inside* your legs, directly under your shoulders.
Keep your chest up, back flat, and shoulders slightly in front of the bar.
Drive through the floor with your legs, keeping the bar close to your body as you stand tall.
Return the bar to the floor by hinging at the hips and bending the knees.
tips: Sumo deadlifts place less stress on the lower back and more on the quads and hips compared to a conventional deadlift.

name: Single-Arm Dumbbell Row
instructions: Place your left knee and left hand on a flat bench, keeping your back flat and parallel to the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in your right hand, letting it hang straight down.
Pull the dumbbell up to your lower ribcage, keeping your elbow close to your side.
Squeeze your back muscles at the top of the movement.
Slowly lower the dumbbell back to a full stretch.
tips: Focus on pulling with your back, not your bicep. Imagine driving your elbow straight up toward the ceiling.

name: Box Jumps
instructions: Stand facing a plyo box, about a foot away, with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Drop into a quarter squat, swinging your arms back to gather momentum.
Explode upward and forward, swinging your arms forward to propel yourself.
Land softly on top of the box with both feet, aiming to land in a partial squat position to absorb the impact.
Stand up fully on the box, then step down carefully (do not jump backward off the box).
tips: Land quietly—if your landing is loud, the box may be too high. Step down instead of jumping down to protect your Achilles tendon.

name: Cable Lateral Raise
instructions: Set a cable pulley to the lowest position and attach a single D-handle.
Stand sideways to the machine and grasp the handle with your outside hand (the one furthest from the machine).
Keep a slight bend in your working arm and raise the cable out to your side until your hand is at shoulder height.
Slowly lower the cable back to the starting position.
tips: The cable provides constant tension throughout the entire range of motion, unlike a dumbbell. You can pass the cable slightly behind or in front of your body.

name: Standing Military Press
instructions: Unrack a barbell from a squat rack at upper-chest height, using a grip slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Stand tall with your feet shoulder-width apart, squeeze your glutes, and brace your core.
Press the bar strictly overhead, moving your head slightly back to let the bar pass your face.
Once the bar clears your head, press it up and slightly backward so it finishes directly over your mid-foot.
Lower the bar back to your upper chest.
tips: Do not use momentum from your legs (that makes it a Push Press). Squeezing your glutes heavily prevents excessive arching of the lower back.

name: Decline Crunch
instructions: Sit on a decline bench and secure your feet firmly under the pads.
Cross your arms over your chest or place your hands lightly behind your ears.
Lower your torso back until you feel a deep stretch in your abs (do not rest completely on the bench).
Contract your abs to curl your torso up toward your knees.
Slowly lower back to the starting position.
tips: The decline angle increases the range of motion and resistance compared to a floor crunch. Don't pull on your neck with your hands.

name: Chin-Up
instructions: Grasp the bar with an underhand grip (palms facing you), hands shoulder-width apart.
Hang freely with your arms fully extended.
Pull yourself up by driving your elbows down toward the floor until your chin clears the bar.
Lower yourself in a controlled manner back to a dead hang.
tips: The underhand grip shifts more focus onto the biceps compared to a standard pull-up. Keep your core tight to prevent swinging.

name: Farmer's Walk
instructions: Stand between two heavy dumbbells or kettlebells.
Squat down with good form to pick them up, and stand up completely straight.
Keep your shoulders pulled back, chest proud, and core braced tightly.
Walk forward using short, quick, measured steps for a set distance or time.
Carefully lower the weights back to the ground by squatting down.
tips: Do not let the weights rest against your legs as you walk. This is a phenomenal exercise for overall work capacity, grip strength, and core stability.

name: Pistol Squat
instructions: Stand on one leg, extending the other leg straight out in front of you.
Extend your arms out in front of you for balance.
Lower your body by bending the knee of your standing leg, keeping your torso as upright as possible.
Descend as far as your mobility allows (ideally until your hamstrings touch your calf).
Drive through the foot of your standing leg to push yourself back up.
tips: This is a very advanced exercise requiring immense strength, balance, and mobility. Modify by holding onto a TRX band or squatting down onto a box.

name: Leg Press Calf Raise
instructions: Sit in a leg press machine and press the sled up until your legs are straight.
Slide your feet down so that only your toes and the balls of your feet are on the bottom edge of the sled. Keep the safety catches engaged if possible.
Let your heels drop back as far as possible to deeply stretch your calves.
Push through your toes to flex your calves, pressing the sled up.
Hold the contraction, then lower slowly.
tips: Keep a very slight micro-bend in your knees; never lock them out completely during this exercise to protect the joint.

name: Decline Push-Up
instructions: Place your toes on a bench or elevated surface and your hands flat on the floor, slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Keep your body in a straight line from your heels to your head.
Lower your chest toward the floor by bending your elbows.
Push back up until your arms are fully extended.
tips: The higher the elevation of your feet, the more this exercise targets your shoulders and upper chest. Do not let your lower back sag.

name: Weighted Pull-Up
instructions: Secure a weight belt around your waist or hold a dumbbell strictly between your feet.
Grasp the pull-up bar with an overhand grip, slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Hang with your arms fully extended.
Pull your chin over the bar by driving your elbows down.
Lower yourself back to a dead hang with control.
tips: Focus on progressive overload by slowly increasing the weight over time. Keep your core tight to prevent the weight from swinging.

name: Scapular Pull-Up
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with an overhand grip and your arms fully extended.
Without bending your elbows, pull your shoulder blades down and together.
Your body will lift slightly upward, but your arms must remain completely straight.
Hold the contraction for a second, then relax back into a dead hang.
tips: This is a foundational movement for mastering the full pull-up and keeping your shoulders healthy. The range of motion is very small.

name: Spiderman Push-Up
instructions: Start in a standard push-up position.
As you lower your body toward the floor, lift your right foot off the ground and bring your right knee to your right elbow.
As you push back up, return your right foot to the starting position.
On the next repetition, bring your left knee to your left elbow.
Continue alternating sides.
tips: This variation heavily engages your core and obliques while adding dynamic movement to the standard push-up. Keep your hips as level as possible.

name: Incline Push-Up
instructions: Place your hands on an elevated surface like a bench, box, or wall, slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Step your feet back so your body forms a straight line.
Lower your chest toward the elevated surface by bending your elbows.
Push back up to the starting position.
tips: This is a great progression exercise for those building up to a standard push-up, as the elevation reduces the amount of body weight you have to lift.

name: Dumbbell Front Raise
instructions: Stand tall holding a dumbbell in each hand resting against your thighs, palms facing you.
Keep a slight bend in your elbows and your core braced.
Raise the dumbbells straight in front of you until your arms are parallel to the floor.
Slowly lower the weights back down to your thighs.
tips: You can perform this with both arms simultaneously or alternate arms. Avoid using momentum by swinging your torso backward.

name: Cable Woodchopper
instructions: Set a cable pulley to the highest position and attach a single handle.
Stand sideways to the machine, feet shoulder-width apart, and grab the handle with both hands.
Keep your arms relatively straight and pull the handle down and across your body toward your opposite knee.
Rotate your torso and pivot your back foot as you bring the weight down.
Slowly return to the starting position under control.
tips: Focus on using your core to perform the rotation, not just your arms. Keep the movement smooth and controlled.

name: Captain's Chair Leg Raise
instructions: Position your forearms on the pads of the chair and grab the handles, with your back pressed flat against the backrest.
Let your legs hang straight down.
Keeping your legs straight, lift them until they are parallel to the floor (or higher).
Slowly lower your legs back down to the starting position without swinging.
tips: If straight legs are too difficult, start by bending your knees and bringing them up toward your chest. Do not let your lower back arch away from the pad.

name: Barbell Walking Lunge
instructions: Rest a barbell across your upper traps, just like a back squat.
Take a large step forward with your right leg.
Lower your hips until your right thigh is parallel to the ground and your left knee is hovering just above the floor.
Push off your right foot to step your left leg forward directly into the next lunge.
Continue walking forward, alternating legs.
tips: Keep your torso perfectly upright. If you struggle with balance, use dumbbells instead before progressing to the barbell.

name: Machine Chest Press
instructions: Sit on the machine and adjust the seat so the handles align with your mid-chest.
Grasp the handles, press your back firmly against the pad, and brace your core.
Push the handles away from you until your arms are fully extended.
Slowly reverse the motion until you feel a stretch across your chest.
tips: Keep your shoulders pinned back against the pad throughout the entire movement. Do not let your shoulders roll forward as you press.

name: Machine Shoulder Press
instructions: Sit on the machine and adjust the seat so the handles are roughly at shoulder level.
Grasp the handles with a pronated (overhand) or neutral grip.
Press the weight straight up until your arms are fully extended without locking your elbows.
Slowly lower the handles back to shoulder level.
tips: This machine provides a fixed range of motion, making it excellent for safely isolating the shoulders without balance requirements.

name: Close-Grip Push-Up
instructions: Assume a push-up position but place your hands close together, directly under your chest. You can form a diamond shape with your thumbs and index fingers if comfortable.
Keep your elbows tucked tightly against your ribs.
Lower your chest toward your hands until you are just above the floor.
Press forcefully back up to full extension.
tips: The closer your hands are, the more your triceps have to work. Keep your body in a rigid straight line.

name: Dumbbell Step-Up
instructions: Stand facing a box or bench, holding a dumbbell in each hand.
Place your right foot completely onto the box.
Drive entirely through your right heel to lift your body up until your right leg is straight. Your left foot should hover next to your right.
Slowly lower your left foot back to the floor, followed by your right foot.
Alternate sides or complete all reps on one side before switching.
tips: Try not to push off the ground with your trailing foot. Make the elevated leg do all the lifting.

name: Kettlebell Goblet Squat
instructions: Hold a kettlebell by the horns (the sides of the handle) right at chest level.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly out.
Keep your chest tall and push your hips back to squat down.
Descend until your elbows touch the inside of your knees (or as deep as your mobility allows).
Drive through your mid-foot to stand back up.
tips: The front-loaded weight forces you to keep an upright torso and deeply engages the core. Great for teaching proper squat mechanics.

name: Glute-Ham Raise (GHR)
instructions: Position yourself on the GHR machine with your knees resting on or just behind the middle pad and your feet secured tightly in the rollers.
Start with your torso upright, forming a 90-degree angle with your legs.
Slowly lower your upper body straight forward until it is parallel to the floor.
Pull yourself back up to the starting position by powerfully contracting your hamstrings and glutes.
tips: Keep your spine neutral throughout. This is one of the most intense hamstring exercises available and may require assistance or band support at first.

name: Landmine Press
equipment: Landmine Attachment (or Corner of a Wall)
instructions: Secure one end of a barbell into a landmine attachment.
Stand facing the attachment and grab the free end of the barbell with one hand, holding it at shoulder height.
Adopt a staggered stance for stability.
Press the barbell forward and upward until your arm is fully extended.
Lower the bar back to your shoulder with control.
tips: The arc of the landmine press is easier on the shoulder joints than a strict vertical overhead press. Keep your core tight to resist twisting.

name: Dumbbell Thruster
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand resting on your shoulders.
Drop into a full front squat, keeping your chest up and elbows high.
Explode out of the squat, using the momentum from your legs to drive the dumbbells straight overhead as you stand.
Lower the dumbbells back to your shoulders smoothly as you descend into the next squat.
tips: This is a fluid, continuous motion. Do not pause between the squat and the press. Excellent for metabolic conditioning.

name: Bench Dips
instructions: Sit on the edge of a bench and place your hands firmly next to your hips, fingers gripping the edge.
Slide your hips off the bench and walk your feet out so your legs are straight (or bent to make it easier).
Lower your body by bending your elbows until they are at a 90-degree angle.
Press back up through your palms until your arms are fully extended.
tips: Keep your back close to the bench as you lower yourself. Going too deep can place unwanted stress on the front of the shoulder.

name: Renegade Row
instructions: Get into a high plank position with a dumbbell in each hand, resting on the floor. Spread your feet wide for balance.
Brace your core to prevent your hips from rotating.
Row the right dumbbell up to your ribcage, balancing your body weight on your left hand and toes.
Lower the right dumbbell back to the floor, then row the left dumbbell up.
Continue alternating arms.
tips: The anti-rotation core component is just as important as the back pull. If your hips are shifting wildly, the weight is too heavy or your feet are too close together.

name: Single-Leg Romanian Deadlift (RDL)
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in your right hand.
Shift your weight onto your left leg, keeping a slight bend in the knee.
Hinge forward at the hips, extending your right leg straight back behind you for balance.
Lower the dumbbell toward the floor until your torso and back leg are parallel to the ground.
Drive your hips forward and squeeze your left glute to return to a standing position.
tips: Keep your back flat and your hips square to the ground; do not let your working hip open up to the side. Hold onto a wall with your free hand if balance is a struggle.

name: Seated Cable Chest Press
instructions: Place an upright bench between two low or mid-height cable pulleys.
Sit on the bench, grab a D-handle in each hand, and bring them to chest height.
Keep your chest proud and your shoulders pinned back against the bench.
Press the cables straight out in front of you until your arms are fully extended.
Slowly return your hands to the starting position.
tips: The cables provide constant tension at the top of the movement, which you lose with dumbbells. Squeeze your chest hard at full extension.

name: Lying Leg Raises
instructions: Lie flat on your back on a mat with your legs fully extended and hands placed under your glutes for lower back support.
Keeping your legs as straight as possible, lift them up toward the ceiling until your hips come slightly off the floor.
Slowly lower your legs back down until they are hovering just an inch above the ground.
Without letting your heels touch the floor, initiate the next repetition.
tips: Press your lower back firmly into the floor. If your back arches, do not lower your legs quite as far down.

name: Reverse Hyperextension
instructions: Lie face down on the machine or a high bench so your hips are at the edge and your legs hang off freely.
Grasp the handles or the sides of the bench to secure your upper body.
Keeping your legs straight, squeeze your glutes to lift your legs backward and upward until they are in line with your torso.
Hold the contraction for a second, then lower your legs back down with control.
tips: This is excellent for decompressing the lower spine while building posterior chain strength. Avoid throwing the weight up with momentum.

name: Cable Glute Kickbacks
instructions: Attach an ankle strap to a low cable pulley and secure it around your right ankle.
Face the machine, holding onto the frame for support, and take a slight step back to create tension.
Keep your torso relatively upright and a slight bend in your working knee.
Kick your right leg straight back, squeezing your glute hard at the top of the movement.
Slowly return the leg to the starting position.
tips: Do not hyperextend your lower back to get your leg higher. The movement should come strictly from the hip extending.

name: Side Plank
instructions: Lie on your side with your legs straight and feet stacked on top of each other.
Prop your upper body up on your forearm, ensuring your elbow is directly underneath your shoulder.
Brace your core and lift your hips off the floor until your body forms a straight line from your head to your feet.
Hold this position for the desired duration, then switch sides.
tips: Do not let your hips sag toward the floor. To modify, you can drop your bottom knee to the ground for extra support.

name: Standing Cable Crunch
instructions: Set a cable pulley to the highest position and attach a rope.
Stand facing away from the machine, grasp the rope overhead, and pull it down so your hands rest behind your neck or by your ears.
With your feet shoulder-width apart, hinge slightly at the hips and lock your lower body in place.
Crunch your torso forward and down, trying to bring your ribcage toward your pelvis.
Slowly return to the upright position, maintaining tension on your abs.
tips: This is a spinal flexion movement, not a hip hinge. Do not pull the weight with your arms or shoulders.

name: Barbell Upright Row
instructions: Stand tall holding a barbell with an overhand grip, hands resting slightly narrower than shoulder-width in front of your thighs.
Keep the bar close to your body and pull it straight up toward your chin, leading with your elbows.
Your elbows should always be higher than your forearms.
Pause when the bar reaches your upper chest or collarbone, then slowly lower it back down.
tips: If you experience shoulder impingement or pain, switch to a wider grip or use dumbbells instead.

name: Sissy Squat
instructions: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, holding onto a solid object next to you for balance if needed.
Rise up onto your toes and lean your torso backward, keeping a straight line from your knees to your shoulders.
Bend your knees forward, lowering your hips toward your heels.
Go as low as your knee mobility allows, feeling a massive stretch in your quads.
Drive back up to the starting position.
tips: Despite the name, this is a grueling and advanced quad isolation exercise. Keep your core tight to prevent your lower back from overarching.

name: Dumbbell Floor Press
instructions: Sit on the floor, pull a pair of dumbbells into your hip creases, and safely lie back, keeping your knees bent and feet flat on the floor.
Press the dumbbells straight up over your chest.
Slowly lower the weights until your triceps completely touch the floor.
Pause for a second, releasing the momentum, then explosively press the dumbbells back up.
tips: The floor limits your range of motion, which removes shoulder strain and places heavy emphasis on building triceps lockout power.

name: Kettlebell Turkish Get-Up
instructions: Lie on your back, holding a kettlebell in your right hand straight up toward the ceiling. Bend your right knee and plant your right foot flat on the floor. Extend your left arm and left leg out at a 45-degree angle.
Push off your right foot and roll onto your left hip and left forearm.
Press up from your forearm onto your left hand.
Bridge your hips high into the air, creating space underneath you.
Sweep your left leg under your body, resting on your left knee (half-kneeling position).
Take your left hand off the floor and sit up tall.
Stand up completely, bringing your feet together.
Reverse all the steps exactly to return to the starting position.
tips: Keep your eyes locked on the kettlebell throughout the entire movement. Do this slowly; it is about control and stability, not speed.

name: Machine Bicep Curl
instructions: Sit at the machine and adjust the seat so your armpits rest comfortably over the top of the pad.
Grasp the handles with an underhand grip.
Keep your triceps flat against the pad and curl the handles up toward your shoulders.
Squeeze your biceps at the peak contraction.
Slowly lower the handles back down until your arms are fully extended.
tips: The machine provides strict isolation by preventing any body English or momentum. Don't let the weight stack slam between reps.

name: Machine Tricep Extension
instructions: Sit down and adjust the seat height so your elbows align with the machine's pivot point.
Rest your upper arms flat on the pad and grasp the handles.
Push the handles down and away from you until your arms are completely straight.
Squeeze your triceps at the bottom.
Slowly return the handles to the starting position.
tips: Ensure your elbows stay firmly planted on the pad and do not flare out during the movement.

name: Straight-Arm Pulldown
instructions: Attach a straight bar or rope to a high cable pulley.
Stand facing the machine, grasp the attachment with an overhand grip, and step back slightly.
Hinge slightly at the hips and keep your arms almost completely straight (just a soft bend in the elbow).
Pull the bar down in an arc motion until it touches your thighs, squeezing your lats hard.
Slowly raise the bar back to eye level.
tips: Focus entirely on using your lats to push the weight down. Do not bend your elbows to turn this into a triceps pushdown.

name: Plate Pinch Hold
instructions: Stand two weight plates of equal size together face-to-face.
Pinch the smooth outside edges of the plates together using your fingers on one side and your thumb on the other.
Stand up straight holding the plates by your side.
Hold them for as long as possible until your grip fails.
Repeat on the other hand.
tips: This is a phenomenal exercise for building pinch grip strength, which is crucial for deadlifts and general hand health.

name: Zercher Squat
instructions: Set a barbell in a squat rack at about waist height.
Position the barbell in the crook of your elbows, clutching it tight to your chest with your hands clasped.
Step back from the rack with a shoulder-width stance.
Keeping your torso extremely upright, squat down until your elbows touch your thighs.
Drive through your feet to stand back up.
tips: This variation heavily taxes the core and upper back due to the front-loaded position. You may want to wear sleeves or use a pad if the bar hurts your elbows.

name: TRX Row (Suspension Row)
instructions: Grab the suspension trainer handles and lean back, walking your feet forward so your body is at an angle.
Keep your body in a perfectly straight line from your head to your heels (like a moving plank).
Start with your arms fully extended.
Pull your chest up to the handles by driving your elbows backward, squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Slowly lower yourself back to the starting position.
tips: To make the exercise harder, walk your feet closer to the anchor point (making your body more horizontal). To make it easier, walk backwards.

name: Swiss Ball Crunch
instructions: Sit on a stability ball and walk your feet forward, rolling the ball out until it supports your lower and middle back.
Plant your feet firmly on the floor, shoulder-width apart.
Place your hands lightly behind your head or cross them over your chest.
Contract your abs to curl your torso upward, raising your shoulders off the ball.
Slowly lower your torso back down to a slight hyperextension over the curve of the ball.
tips: The ball allows for a greater range of motion than a floor crunch, deeply stretching the abs at the bottom of the movement.

name: Wall Sit
instructions: Stand with your back flat against a wall.
Walk your feet out about two feet in front of you.
Slide your back down the wall until your knees are bent at a 90-degree angle, making your thighs parallel to the floor.
Keep your back flat against the wall and hold this isometric position for the desired time.
tips: Do not rest your hands on your thighs. This is an excellent isometric burnout for the quads at the end of a leg workout.

name: B-Stance Romanian Deadlift
instructions: Stand holding dumbbells or a barbell, and shift your stance so your right foot is slightly behind your left foot.
Pop your right heel off the ground, resting only on the toes for balance (the 'B-Stance').
Keep 80% of your weight on your flat, front (left) foot.
Hinge at the hips, keeping your back flat, pushing your glutes backward until you feel a deep stretch in your left hamstring.
Drive your hips forward to stand back up.
tips: This offers the benefits of a single-leg RDL without the immense balance requirement, allowing you to load the hamstring much heavier.

name: Seated Dumbbell Shrugs
instructions: Sit on the edge of a flat bench holding a dumbbell in each hand, arms fully extended down by your sides.
Sit up very tall, keep your chest proud, and your feet flat on the floor.
Without bending your elbows, elevate your shoulders straight up toward your ears.
Squeeze your traps at the top for a full second.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back down to a dead hang.
tips: Sitting removes the ability to use your legs to cheat or bounce the weight up, forcing the traps to do all the work.

name: Kettlebell Snatch
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a kettlebell in one hand between your legs.
Hinge at the hips and hike the kettlebell backward.
Explosively drive your hips forward to propel the kettlebell upward, keeping it close to your body.
As the bell passes your face, quickly punch your hand upward and let the bell flip softly onto the back of your wrist.
Finish with your arm locked out directly overhead, then safely drop the bell back down between your legs in a fluid motion.
tips: This is a highly technical, explosive movement. Master the single-arm kettlebell swing and the kettlebell clean before attempting the snatch to prevent wrist banging.

name: Decline Dumbbell Bench Press
instructions: Secure your feet at the top of a decline bench and safely lie back with a dumbbell in each hand.
Press the dumbbells straight up above your lower chest with your palms facing forward.
Slowly lower the dumbbells to the outside of your chest, keeping a 45-degree angle in your elbows.
Press the dumbbells back up to the starting position.
tips: Dumbbells allow for a deeper stretch and a more natural range of motion than a barbell. Always use a spotter when lifting heavy on a decline.

name: Cable Pull-Through
instructions: Attach a rope handle to a low pulley on a cable machine.
Stand facing away from the machine, reach between your legs to grab the rope, and step forward to create tension.
With a wide stance and slightly bent knees, hinge at your hips, pushing your glutes backward.
Let the cable pull your hands through your legs until you feel a deep stretch in your hamstrings.
Drive your hips forward and squeeze your glutes hard to stand up straight.
tips: This is a horizontal hip hinge. Keep your arms completely straight; they are just hooks holding the weight, not pulling it.

name: Chest-Supported Dumbbell Row
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30 to 45-degree incline.
Lie face down on the bench with a dumbbell in each hand, letting your arms hang straight down to the floor.
Pull the dumbbells up toward your ribcage, squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Lower the weights slowly and with control until your arms are fully extended again.
tips: The chest support completely removes the lower back from the equation, allowing you to strictly isolate the upper back and lats.

name: Zottman Curl
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand by your sides, palms facing forward (supinated grip).
Curl the weights up toward your shoulders, squeezing your biceps.
At the top of the movement, rotate your wrists 180 degrees so your palms are now facing downward (pronated grip).
Slowly lower the dumbbells back down to the starting position using the overhand grip.
Rotate your wrists back to the starting, palms-up position.
tips: This exercise is incredible for hitting both the biceps on the concentric (lifting) phase and the forearms on the eccentric (lowering) phase.

name: Hanging Knee Raise
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with a shoulder-width, overhand grip.
Keeping your torso completely still, pull your knees upward toward your chest.
Squeeze your abs tight at the top of the movement.
Slowly lower your legs back down to a dead hang without letting your body swing.
tips: Tuck your pelvis upward at the top of the movement to fully engage the lower abdominals. Bending the knees makes this easier than a strict straight-leg raise.

name: Single-Leg Calf Raise
instructions: Stand with the ball of one foot on the edge of a step, weight plate, or block. Let your heel hang off.
Tuck your other foot behind your working leg. Hold onto a wall or rail for balance if necessary.
Lower your working heel toward the floor as far as you can to feel a deep stretch.
Push up onto the toes of your working foot as high as possible, flexing the calf.
Lower back down slowly.
tips: Working unilaterally (one side at a time) ensures that your stronger leg isn't compensating for your weaker one.

name: Dumbbell Forward Lunge
instructions: Stand tall holding a dumbbell in each hand by your sides.
Take a large, controlled step forward with your right foot.
Lower your hips straight down until both knees are bent at a 90-degree angle.
Your back knee should lightly tap or hover just above the floor.
Push forcefully off your right foot to step back to the starting, standing position.
Alternate legs.
tips: Forward lunges place slightly more emphasis on the quads and require more deceleration strength than reverse lunges.

name: Kettlebell Clean and Press
instructions: Stand over a kettlebell with a shoulder-width stance. Hinge down and grab the handle with one hand.
Explosively extend your hips to pull the kettlebell up, pulling your elbow back and keeping the bell close to your body.
Rotate your hand slightly so the kettlebell flips smoothly to rest against the back of your forearm at shoulder height (the rack position).
From the rack position, press the kettlebell straight up overhead until your arm is fully extended.
Lower the kettlebell back to your shoulder, then carefully drop it back to the floor.
tips: This is a two-part movement. Master the clean before adding the press. The bell should not slam into your wrist during the clean.

name: Pallof Press
instructions: Set a cable pulley or band to chest height.
Stand perfectly perpendicular to the anchor point, holding the handle with both hands at the center of your chest.
Step out to create tension in the cable/band.
Brace your core tightly and press the handle straight out in front of you until your arms are fully extended.
Hold for a second, fighting the rotational pull of the cable, then slowly bring your hands back to your chest.
tips: This is an anti-rotation exercise. Your goal is to keep your torso perfectly still while the weight tries to twist you toward the machine.

name: Sled Push
instructions: Load the sled with the desired weight.
Grip the poles with straight arms (or place your forearms on the poles for a lower profile).
Lean into the sled so your body is at a 45-degree angle.
Drive your legs like pistons, taking powerful, deliberate steps to push the sled across the turf.
Keep your back flat and core tight as you move.
tips: The sled push offers zero eccentric (lowering) load, meaning you can train incredibly hard with very little muscle soreness the next day.

name: Swiss Ball Pike
instructions: Start in a high plank position with your hands on the floor and your shins/feet resting securely on top of a stability ball.
Keep your legs completely straight and pull your hips up toward the ceiling.
The ball will roll forward under your toes as your body forms an inverted 'V' shape.
Pause at the top, contracting your abs hard.
Slowly lower your hips back down to the starting plank position.
tips: If this is too difficult, bend your knees and pull the ball into your chest (a Swiss Ball Knee Tuck) instead.

name: Machine Lateral Raise
instructions: Sit on the machine with your chest against the pad (or back against the pad, depending on the machine design).
Position your upper arms against the pads or grasp the handles.
Push the weight outward and upward using the sides of your shoulders until your upper arms are parallel to the floor.
Slowly control the weight back down to the starting position.
tips: The machine provides constant tension, which is superior to dumbbells at the very bottom of the range of motion.

name: Barbell Hack Squat
instructions: Stand with a barbell on the floor directly behind your calves.
Keep a shoulder-width stance and squat down, keeping your torso as upright as possible.
Grasp the barbell behind your back with an overhand grip.
Drive through your heels to stand straight up, pulling the bar up the back of your legs.
Squat back down, returning the bar to the floor.
tips: This old-school variation heavily targets the quads similarly to a hack squat machine, but requires significant grip strength.

name: Copenhagen Plank
instructions: Lie on your side perpendicular to a bench.
Place your top foot and ankle squarely on top of the bench.
Prop your upper body up on your bottom forearm (like a side plank).
Lift your hips off the ground until your body is in a straight line, leaving your bottom leg hovering off the floor.
Hold this position, using your top inner thigh to support your body weight.
tips: This is an intense isometric hold for the adductors. To make it easier, place your knee on the bench instead of your foot.

name: Dumbbell Deadlift
instructions: Stand tall holding a dumbbell in each hand, resting in front of your thighs or by your sides.
Push your hips backward and bend your knees slightly.
Keep your back perfectly flat and chest up as you lower the dumbbells toward the floor.
Tap the dumbbells to the floor outside your feet (or go as low as your mobility safely allows).
Drive through your feet and push your hips forward to return to standing.
tips: Dumbbells are a great way to learn the hip hinge pattern before moving to a barbell. Keep the weights close to your center of gravity.

name: Deficit Deadlift
equipment: Low Platform or Weight Plate to stand on
instructions: Stand on a sturdy, slightly elevated platform (1-3 inches high, like a bumper plate).
Set up for a conventional deadlift, gripping the bar outside your legs.
Because you are elevated, you will need to sink your hips slightly deeper to reach the bar.
Brace your core and drive through the floor to lift the barbell, locking out your hips at the top.
Return the bar to the floor with control.
tips: The deficit increases the range of motion, placing more emphasis on quad drive off the floor. Use lighter weight than your standard deadlift.

name: Single-Arm Cable Row
instructions: Set a cable pulley to chest height and attach a D-handle.
Grab the handle with one hand and step back to create tension, standing in a split stance for balance.
Start with your arm fully extended, allowing a stretch in your lat.
Pull the handle straight back to your ribcage, keeping your elbow tucked close to your side.
Rotate your torso slightly at the end to get a full squeeze, then return to the start.
tips: Working one arm at a time allows for a slightly greater range of motion and core engagement to prevent rotation.

name: Body Saw
instructions: Start in a forearm plank position with your feet resting on sliders (or a towel on a smooth floor).
Squeeze your glutes and brace your core so your body forms a perfectly rigid straight line.
Using your shoulders, push your body backward, letting your feet slide out behind you.
Slide back as far as you can while maintaining a flat lower back.
Pull yourself forward back to the starting position.
tips: This acts similarly to an ab wheel rollout but from the toes. Do not let your hips drop or spike into the air.

name: Barbell Shrugs
instructions: Stand holding a barbell in front of your thighs with an overhand grip, hands placed slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Keep your arms completely straight and your torso upright.
Elevate your shoulders straight up toward your ears as high as possible.
Hold the peak contraction for a second, then lower the bar back to the starting position.
tips: Do not roll your shoulders in circles. You can use lifting straps if your grip fails before your traps do.

name: Z-Press
instructions: Sit flat on the floor with your legs extended straight out in front of you, spread in a slight V-shape.
Hold a barbell or a pair of dumbbells at upper-chest level.
Sit up completely straight, bracing your core hard to prevent yourself from falling backward.
Press the weight strictly overhead until your arms are fully locked out.
Lower the weight back to your chest under control.
tips: Because you have no leg drive or back support, this exercise demands massive core strength and strict shoulder mobility. Start very light.

name: Ring Dips
instructions: Jump or step up to support yourself on the rings with your arms fully extended and elbows locked.
Turn your knuckles outward slightly to stabilize the rings against your body.
Lower yourself by bending your elbows, keeping them tucked relatively close to your sides.
Descend until your shoulders drop below your elbows.
Push powerfully back up to the starting position.
tips: The instability of the rings makes this exponentially harder than parallel bar dips. Keep your core tight to prevent violent swinging.

name: Cossack Squat
instructions: Stand with an extremely wide stance, toes pointed slightly outward.
Shift your weight to your right leg, bending your right knee and pushing your hips backward to squat down on that side.
Keep your left leg completely straight, letting your left toes point up toward the ceiling as you descend.
Keep your right heel firmly planted on the floor and your chest upright.
Drive through your right foot to stand back up to the wide stance, then repeat on the left side.
tips: This is a phenomenal mobility and unilateral strength exercise. Only go as deep as you can while keeping your working heel flat on the floor.

name: Nordic Hamstring Curl
equipment: Partner or Fixed Object to anchor feet
instructions: Kneel on a soft pad and have a partner firmly hold your ankles down (or hook your heels securely under a heavy, stable object).
Keep your body in a perfectly straight line from your knees to your head.
Slowly lower your torso forward toward the floor, using your hamstrings to resist the fall.
Control the descent for as long as possible until you catch yourself on the floor with your hands.
Push off the floor lightly with your hands to help pull yourself back up to the starting position.
tips: This is heavily focused on the eccentric (lengthening) phase of the hamstring. It is notorious for causing intense soreness, so ease into it.

name: Incline Dumbbell Curl
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 45 to 60-degree incline.
Lie back on the bench with a dumbbell in each hand, letting your arms hang straight down toward the floor, palms facing forward.
Keep your upper arms perfectly still and pointing toward the floor.
Curl both dumbbells up toward your shoulders, squeezing your biceps.
Slowly lower the weights back to a full dead hang.
tips: Sitting on an incline positions your arms behind your torso, which deeply stretches the long head of the bicep. Do not let your elbows drift forward as you curl.

name: Decline Sit-Up
instructions: Sit on a decline bench and secure your feet securely under the roller pads.
Cross your arms over your chest or place your fingertips lightly behind your ears.
Lower your upper body backward until your back is almost touching the bench.
Contract your core to pull your torso all the way up until it is perpendicular to the floor.
Slowly lower yourself back down with control.
tips: The decline angle forces your abs to work through a larger range of motion against gravity. Do not yank your neck forward with your hands.

name: Kettlebell Halo
instructions: Stand tall or kneel on one or both knees. Hold a kettlebell upside down by the horns (bottom pointing up) right in front of your chest.
Brace your core tight so your torso does not move.
Slowly circle the kettlebell closely around the right side of your head, bringing it behind your neck, and then around the left side back to the front.
Keep the kettlebell as close to your head as possible without hitting yourself.
Alternate directions with each repetition.
tips: This is an exceptional shoulder mobility and warm-up exercise. Your head and torso should remain entirely motionless; only your arms move.

name: Meadows Row
instructions: Secure one end of a barbell in a landmine attachment or corner. Load the other end with smaller plates (e.g., 25s instead of 45s) to increase range of motion.
Stand perpendicular to the loaded end of the bar in a staggered stance (working arm side leg back).
Hinge at the hips and grab the thick end of the barbell (the sleeve) with an overhand grip.
Row the barbell up toward your back hip, letting your elbow flare out slightly to target the upper lats and rear delts.
Lower the weight back to a full stretch.
tips: Using a lifting strap is highly recommended because gripping the thick sleeve of the barbell will quickly fatigue your forearms.

name: Barbell Floor Press
instructions: Set the J-hooks in a power rack low to the ground and place a barbell on them.
Lie flat on the floor directly under the bar, with your knees bent and feet planted flat.
Grip the bar slightly narrower than your standard bench press grip and unrack it.
Lower the bar slowly until your triceps (the backs of your upper arms) come to a complete dead stop on the floor.
Pause for a split second, then forcefully press the bar back up to lockout.
tips: The floor eliminates leg drive and cuts off the bottom half of the range of motion, making it a pure upper-body pressing and tricep-building exercise.

name: Suitcase Carry
instructions: Stand next to a heavy dumbbell or kettlebell. Squat down with a flat back and pick it up with one hand.
Stand completely upright. Your goal is to look as though you are holding nothing—do not lean away from the weight or let the weight pull you to the side.
Keep your shoulders level and your core intensely braced.
Walk forward slowly and deliberately for a set time or distance.
Carefully lower the weight, switch hands, and repeat.
tips: This is a moving side plank. It builds incredible anti-lateral flexion core strength and grip stability.

name: Trap Bar Deadlift (Hex Bar Deadlift)
instructions: Step inside the center of the loaded trap bar with your feet hip-width apart.
Push your hips backward and bend your knees to grab the handles firmly.
Drop your hips, lift your chest, keep your back flat, and look straight ahead.
Take a deep breath, brace your core, and drive through the floor with your legs to stand up straight with the weight.
Hinge at the hips and bend the knees to lower the bar back to a dead stop on the floor.
tips: The trap bar aligns the weight directly with your center of gravity, putting less shear force on the lower back and utilizing more quadriceps than a conventional barbell deadlift.

name: Donkey Calf Raise
equipment: Donkey Calf Raise Machine (or a weight belt/partner)
instructions: Position yourself in the machine with your toes on the edge of the platform and the pad resting securely over your lower back/hips.
Bend at the hips so your torso is roughly parallel to the floor. Keep your legs straight with soft knees.
Drop your heels down as far as they can go to stretch your calves.
Push forcefully up onto your toes, contracting your calves at the top.
Lower back down under control.
tips: Bending over stretches the hamstrings and gastrocnemius, often resulting in a deeper contraction. If you lack the machine, you can perform this with a dip belt loaded with plates.

name: Machine Glute Kickback
instructions: Adjust the chest pad or handles so your body is stabilized on the machine.
Place the bottom of your working foot flat against the push-pad or roller.
Keeping a slight bend in your working knee, press the pad backward until your leg is fully extended.
Squeeze your glute intensely at the top of the extension.
Slowly bring your leg back to the starting position without letting the weight stack rest.
tips: Do not hyperextend your lower back. If you feel this entirely in your lower back, lower the weight and focus purely on pushing from the hip.

name: Cable Bicep Curl
instructions: Attach a bar to the lowest pulley on a cable machine.
Stand facing the machine, grasp the bar with an underhand (supinated) grip, and take a small step backward.
Keep your elbows tucked into your sides and your shoulders pulled back.
Curl the bar up toward your shoulders, squeezing your biceps.
Slowly lower the bar back to full extension, resisting the pull of the cable.
tips: Cables offer constant, uninterrupted tension throughout the entire range of motion, which is a great advantage over free weights for hypertrophy.

name: Pike Push-Up
instructions: Start in a standard push-up position.
Walk your feet forward and hike your hips high up into the air so your body forms an inverted 'V' shape.
Keep your legs straight and position your hands slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Lower your head straight down toward the floor, slightly in front of your hands, by bending your elbows.
Push forcefully back up until your arms are fully extended.
tips: This simulates an overhead press using bodyweight. The higher your hips, the more it targets the shoulders rather than the chest.

name: Burpee
instructions: Stand tall with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Drop quickly into a squat position and place your hands flat on the floor in front of you.
Kick both feet back explosively so you land in a high plank position.
Perform one complete push-up, touching your chest to the floor.
Jump your feet forward, back to the squat position.
Explode straight up into the air, jumping as high as you can and reaching your arms overhead.
Land softly and immediately transition into the next repetition.
tips: This is a full-body conditioning movement. To modify and make it easier, omit the push-up and the final jump (often called a 'Sprawl').

name: Bear Crawl
instructions: Start on all fours with your hands directly under your shoulders and your knees directly under your hips.
Lift your knees exactly one inch off the floor, balancing on your toes and hands.
Keep your back perfectly flat (imagine a glass of water resting on it).
Move forward by simultaneously stepping your right hand and left foot forward.
Follow immediately with your left hand and right foot, continuing in a crawling motion.
tips: The lower you keep your hips and knees to the floor, the harder your core and quads have to work. Do not let your hips pike up into the air.

name: Superman Hold
