instructions: Lie face down on a mat with your legs straight and arms extended fully in front of you.
Squeeze your glutes and lower back muscles to simultaneously lift your arms, chest, and legs off the floor.
Only your stomach and hips should remain in contact with the ground.
Hold this arched position for the desired amount of time.
Slowly lower back to the floor.
tips: Keep your neck in a neutral position by looking down at the floor, not up at the wall in front of you.

name: Banded Pull-Apart
instructions: Stand tall holding a resistance band with an overhand grip, hands roughly shoulder-width apart.
Raise your arms straight out in front of you at chest height.
Keeping your arms completely straight, pull the band apart by squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Continue pulling until your arms are straight out to your sides and the band touches your chest.
Slowly reverse the motion, resisting the snap of the band, back to the starting position.
tips: This is one of the best exercises for shoulder health and posture. Keep your shoulders pulled down away from your ears.

name: Bicycle Crunches
instructions: Lie flat on your back, pressing your lower back into the floor. Place your fingertips lightly behind your ears.
Lift your legs off the floor, bending your knees to a 90-degree angle.
Lift your head and shoulders off the ground.
Extend your right leg straight out (hovering above the floor) while simultaneously twisting your torso to bring your right elbow toward your left knee.
Immediately switch sides, extending the left leg and bringing your left elbow toward your right knee.
Continue alternating in a smooth, pedaling motion.
tips: Focus on rotating your entire torso to move your shoulder toward the knee, not just flapping your elbows back and forth.

name: GHD Sit-Up
instructions: Sit on the GHD machine with your glutes hanging slightly off the main pad and your feet securely locked into the rollers.
Start with your torso upright and your knees slightly bent.
Extend your legs to straight and lower your torso backward until it is parallel to the floor (or slightly below, depending on mobility).
Explosively contract your abs and hip flexors to pull yourself back up to the seated position.
tips: This exercise places significant stress on the hip flexors and lumbar spine. Master standard decline sit-ups before attempting this, and control your descent.

name: Dumbbell Snatch
instructions: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, with a dumbbell resting on the floor between them.
Push your hips back and squat down to grab the dumbbell with one hand using an overhand grip.
Keep your chest up and back flat.
Explosively drive through your feet and extend your hips, pulling the dumbbell straight up along your body.
As the dumbbell reaches chest height, drop under it slightly and punch your arm straight up to catch it overhead.
Stand up fully with the arm locked out, then carefully return the weight to the floor.
tips: The power comes from the hips, not the arm. Keep the dumbbell as close to your body as possible during the pull.

name: Machine Crunch
instructions: Sit in the machine and adjust the seat so the pivot point aligns with your stomach.
Grasp the handles or rest your arms on the pads (depending on the machine design).
Tuck your chin slightly and contract your abs to fold your torso forward, bringing your chest toward your knees.
Squeeze your abs at the bottom of the movement.
Slowly return to the starting position, resisting the weight stack.
tips: Focus on curving your spine forward rather than hinging at the hips. Do not use your arms to pull the weight down.

name: Single-Arm Kettlebell Swing
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, with the kettlebell slightly in front of you.
Hinge at the hips and grasp the kettlebell handle with one hand.
Hike the kettlebell back between your legs.
Explosively drive your hips forward to propel the kettlebell up to chest height.
Let the kettlebell fall back down between your legs naturally and immediately transition into the next rep.
tips: Your free arm should swing rhythmically with the working arm. The anti-rotation challenge to the core is significantly higher here than in the two-handed version.

name: Barbell Ab Rollout
instructions: Load a barbell with small, round plates (like 10s or 25s) and kneel on a mat behind it.
Grab the barbell with a shoulder-width overhand grip.
Lock your arms and brace your core so your body forms a straight line from your knees to your shoulders.
Slowly roll the barbell forward, lowering your hips and torso together toward the floor.
Roll out as far as you can without letting your lower back sag.
Pull the barbell back to your knees using your lats and core.
tips: This is identical in function to an ab wheel but allows for a wider, more stable grip. Never let your lower back hyperextend.

name: Machine Pullover
instructions: Adjust the seat so the machine's pivot point lines up with your shoulder joints.
Sit down, press the foot pedal to bring the bar forward (if applicable), and place your elbows on the pads while grabbing the bar.
Release the foot pedal and let the weight pull your arms up and back to get a deep stretch in the lats.
Pull your elbows forward and down in an arc until the bar reaches your lap.
Slowly let your arms raise back to the starting stretched position.
tips: This machine is a rare gem that allows you to isolate the lats without relying on your biceps at all. Focus the pull entirely through your elbows.

name: High Pulley Cable Curl
instructions: Set two opposing cable pulleys to a height just above your shoulders and attach a D-handle to each.
Stand exactly in the middle of the station and grab a handle in each hand with your palms facing the ceiling.
Keep your upper arms parallel to the floor and stationary.
Curl your hands toward your ears, squeezing your biceps hard at the peak contraction.
Slowly let your arms extend back out.
tips: Commonly known as the 'front double bicep curl', this exercise maintains constant tension on the muscle at the peak contraction.

name: Muscle-Up
instructions: Hang from the bar using a false grip (palms resting over the top of the bar) to make the transition easier.
Perform an explosive pull-up, driving your chest higher than the bar.
As your chest reaches the bar, swiftly throw your head and shoulders forward to transition your body weight over the top of the bar.
Immediately press yourself up until your arms are fully extended (the dip portion).
Lower yourself back down with control.
tips: This is a highly advanced gymnastics movement requiring immense explosive pulling power. Master weighted pull-ups and straight-bar dips before attempting.

name: L-Sit
instructions: Sit on the floor or between parallettes with your legs completely straight out in front of you.
Place your hands on the parallettes or flat on the floor right next to your hips.
Lock your arms out and push your shoulders down to lift your glutes off the floor.
Engage your core and quads to lift your straight legs off the ground so your body forms an 'L' shape.
Hold this position for as long as possible.
tips: If lifting straight legs is too difficult, start by practicing the tuck hold (lifting your glutes off the floor with your knees tucked into your chest).

name: Snatch-Grip Deadlift
instructions: Stand with the barbell over your mid-foot. Take an exceptionally wide grip on the bar (index fingers outside the outermost ring markings).
Because of the wide grip, you will need to sink your hips much deeper than a conventional deadlift to reach the bar.
Keep your chest up, back flat, and shoulders over the bar.
Drive through the floor with your legs, keeping the bar close to your shins, and stand up completely.
Lower the bar back to the floor with control.
tips: The wide grip severely increases the range of motion (acting like a deficit deadlift) and intensely taxes the upper back to keep the shoulders from rounding.

name: Waiter's Walk
instructions: Clean and press a kettlebell or dumbbell strictly overhead with one hand.
Lock your elbow out completely and keep your bicep close to your ear.
Pull your ribcage down, brace your core, and stand perfectly upright.
Walk forward slowly and deliberately, keeping the weight stable overhead for a set distance or time.
Carefully lower the weight, switch sides, and repeat.
tips: If your elbow keeps bending, the weight is too heavy or your shoulder mobility is lacking. Keep your eyes looking straight ahead.

name: Clamshells
instructions: Lie on your side with your hips and knees bent to a 45-degree angle. Stack your legs perfectly on top of each other.
Rest your head on your lower arm.
Keeping your feet in contact with each other, slowly raise your top knee as high as you can without rolling your pelvis backward.
Squeeze your outer glute at the top of the movement.
Slowly lower the knee back to the starting position.
tips: Wrap a resistance band right above your knees to dramatically increase the difficulty. Do not let your lower back do the rotating.

name: Jefferson Curl
instructions: Stand on the edge of a box or bench holding a very light weight in front of your thighs.
Keep your legs completely straight (locked out).
Tuck your chin to your chest and slowly roll your spine downward, one vertebra at a time, starting from the neck down to the lower back.
Let the weight pull you down past your toes into a deep hamstring stretch.
Slowly reverse the motion, unrolling your spine from the bottom up until you are standing tall again.
tips: Unlike a deadlift, the goal here IS to round your back safely under very light load. It builds immense segmental spinal mobility and resilience.

name: Spider Curl
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 45-degree incline.
Lie face down on the bench holding dumbbells or a bar, letting your arms hang straight down to the floor.
Keep your upper arms completely still and pointing directly at the floor.
Curl the weight straight up toward your face, squeezing the biceps hard at the top.
Lower the weight back to a dead hang with control.
tips: Similar to a preacher curl, this exercise prevents any cheating or momentum by locking the torso against the bench.

name: TRX Push-Up
instructions: Adjust the suspension straps so the handles are roughly a foot off the ground.
Grab the handles and get into a push-up position, walking your feet back so your body is straight.
Keep your core extremely tight to prevent the straps from swaying.
Lower your chest toward the handles by bending your elbows.
Push back up to the starting position until your arms are straight.
tips: The instability forces the chest and core muscles to work much harder just to balance the movement. Lower the strap height or elevate your feet to increase difficulty.

name: Wall Walks
instructions: Start in a push-up position with your feet touching the base of a wall.
Perform a push-up, and as you come up, take a step up the wall with your feet.
Begin walking your hands backward toward the wall while simultaneously walking your feet higher up the wall.
Continue until your chest is very close to or touching the wall (in a handstand position).
Carefully walk your hands forward and your feet back down the wall to return to the starting position.
tips: This is a grueling shoulder conditioning and core strength exercise. Do not let your lower back excessively arch when you are inverted.

name: Sandbag Carry
instructions: Stand over a heavy sandbag.
Squat down, weave your arms securely underneath it, and pull it tightly into your stomach/chest.
Stand up tall, leaning slightly back to counterbalance the front-loaded weight.
Squeeze the bag hard against your torso and walk forward with short, measured steps.
Carefully drop or lower the bag to the floor once the distance is covered.
tips: The shifting nature of the sand makes this infinitely more difficult than carrying a static weight. Breathe shallowly into your chest since the bag is compressing your diaphragm.

name: Medicine Ball Slam
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart holding a slam ball with both hands.
Rise up onto your toes, reach the ball high overhead, and fully extend your entire body.
Explosively hinge at the hips, throw your arms down, and slam the ball into the floor directly in front of you with as much force as possible.
Squat down to retrieve the ball as it drops, and immediately initiate the next rep.
tips: Do not just drop the ball; intentionally try to break the floor with it using your lats and core. Ensure you use a dead-blow ball so it doesn't bounce back into your face.

name: Kettlebell Windmill
instructions: Clean and press a kettlebell overhead with your right hand. Lock your elbow and keep your eyes on the bell.
Point both feet to the left at about a 45-degree angle.
Push your right hip back and out to the side.
Keeping your right arm pointing straight up at the ceiling, trace your left hand down your left leg toward the floor.
Go as deep as your flexibility allows, keeping your right leg straight (the left knee can bend slightly).
Squeeze your glutes and obliques to stand back up, keeping the bell directly overhead.
tips: This requires incredible shoulder stability and hamstring flexibility. Keep your eyes glued to the weight overhead the entire time.

name: TRX Pistol Squat
instructions: Hold the TRX handles with your elbows bent and tucked at your sides, facing the anchor point.
Stand on one leg, extending the other leg straight out in front of you.
Lower your body down into a single-leg squat, keeping your chest up and your weight in your heel.
Use the TRX straps for balance and to assist you through the sticking point.
Drive through your foot and pull slightly on the straps to stand back up.
tips: This is a fantastic progression to build the strength and mobility required for a freestanding pistol squat. Rely on your leg to do the pushing, using your arms only as much as necessary.

name: Inverted Row
equipment: Barbell in a Rack or Smith Machine
instructions: Set a barbell in a power rack or Smith machine at about waist height.
Position yourself under the bar, face up, and grasp it with an overhand grip slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Walk your feet forward so your body is at an angle, resting on your heels. Keep your body in a straight line.
Pull your chest up to the bar by driving your elbows backward and retracting your shoulder blades.
Slowly lower yourself back to the starting position with your arms fully extended.
tips: To make the exercise harder, lower the bar or elevate your feet on a bench. Keep your core tight to prevent your hips from sagging.

name: Cable Tricep Kickback
instructions: Set a cable pulley to the lowest position. You can use a single D-handle or just grab the rubber stopper on the cable.
Bend forward at the hips so your torso is almost parallel to the floor, keeping a flat back.
Tuck your working elbow firmly against your ribs and bring your upper arm parallel to the floor.
Extend your elbow back until your arm is completely straight, squeezing the tricep hard.
Slowly let the cable pull your hand back to the 90-degree angle.
tips: The cable provides constant tension through the whole movement, fixing the 'dead zone' at the bottom of a dumbbell kickback. Do not swing your shoulder.

name: Smith Machine Shrug
instructions: Set the Smith machine bar to mid-thigh height.
Stand tall and grip the bar with a shoulder-width overhand grip. Unrack the bar.
Keeping your arms completely straight, shrug your shoulders straight up toward your ears.
Hold the contraction at the top for a full second.
Slowly lower the bar back to the starting position.
tips: The Smith machine locks the bar in a fixed vertical path, allowing you to load the traps extremely heavy without worrying about balancing the bar.

name: High Stance Leg Press
instructions: Sit on the leg press machine and place your feet high up on the sled, shoulder-width apart.
Push the sled up to unrack it and release the safety handles.
Lower the sled by bending your knees until they reach a 90-degree angle, or until your lower back begins to round.
Drive through your heels to push the sled back up without fully locking your knees.
tips: Placing your feet higher on the platform shifts the emphasis heavily away from the quads and onto the glutes and hamstrings.

name: Machine Preacher Curl
instructions: Adjust the seat height so your armpits rest comfortably over the top edge of the pad.
Sit down, rest your triceps flat against the pad, and grasp the handles.
Curl the handles up toward your face, squeezing your biceps hard at the peak.
Lower the weight slowly and with control until your arms are nearly fully extended.
tips: Do not completely hyperextend your elbows at the bottom of the movement while under heavy load. The pad isolates the biceps perfectly by eliminating momentum.

name: Low-to-High Cable Crossover
instructions: Set two opposing cable pulleys to the lowest floor setting and attach D-handles.
Stand in the middle, grab a handle in each hand, and step forward to create tension.
Keep a slight bend in your elbows and your palms facing forward/upward.
Sweep the handles upward and inward in a wide arc until they meet in front of your upper chest/face.
Slowly lower the handles back down to your sides.
tips: This movement mimics an incline bench press and explicitly targets the upper chest fibers. Think about scooping the weight up.

name: Smith Machine Hip Thrust
instructions: Place a bench horizontally behind the Smith machine bar.
Sit on the floor with your upper back against the bench and roll the padded bar over your hips.
Plant your feet flat on the floor, roughly shoulder-width apart.
Drive through your heels, unrack the bar, and push your hips toward the ceiling.
Squeeze your glutes at the top so your thighs and torso are parallel to the floor, then lower back down.
tips: The Smith machine makes setting up much easier than a free barbell and provides a fixed plane of motion so you can focus 100% on the glute squeeze.

name: Front Plate Raise
instructions: Stand tall holding a single weight plate with both hands at the 3 o'clock and 9 o'clock positions.
Let the plate rest against your thighs with a slight bend in your elbows.
Keeping your core braced and arms relatively straight, raise the plate in front of you until it reaches eye level.
Pause for a second, then lower the plate back down slowly.
tips: Using a single plate locks your hands in place and forces your front delts to work together symmetrically. Do not swing your back to get the weight up.

name: Kettlebell Figure 8
instructions: Stand with your feet slightly wider than shoulder-width, holding a kettlebell in your right hand.
Drop into a partial squat, keeping your chest up and back flat.
Pass the kettlebell through your legs from the front to the back, grabbing it with your left hand behind your left leg.
Bring the kettlebell around the outside of your left leg to the front, then pass it through your legs again to your right hand behind your right leg.
Continue tracing this 'Figure 8' pattern continuously.
tips: This is a fantastic dynamic core and hip stabilization exercise. Keep your weight on your heels and your chest up the entire time.

name: Banded Face Pull
instructions: Secure a resistance band to an anchor point at face level.
Grab the band with both hands, palms facing each other, and step back until there is tension on the band.
Stand tall and pull the band straight toward your face, separating your hands as they approach your head.
Squeeze your upper back and rear delts, keeping your elbows higher than your wrists.
Slowly return to the starting position.
tips: The band provides increasing resistance as it stretches, heavily taxing the end range of motion. This is an incredible posture-correcting movement.

name: Sled Pull (Backward)
instructions: Attach straps or a harness to a loaded sled.
Face the sled, hold the straps with your arms extended, and lean backward so your bodyweight acts as a counterbalance.
Keep your chest up and squat down slightly.
Take powerful backward steps, driving through the balls of your feet to pull the sled toward you.
Continue walking backward for the desired distance.
tips: Walking backward with resistance is one of the single best exercises for bulletproofing the knees and building immense quad strength without eccentric joint strain.

name: Smith Machine Reverse Lunge
instructions: Set the Smith machine bar to a comfortable height across your upper traps and unrack it.
Stand with your feet together.
Take a large, controlled step backward with your right leg.
Lower your hips straight down until your left thigh is parallel to the ground and your right knee hovers just above the floor.
Drive entirely through your left heel to return to a standing position. Alternate legs.
tips: The fixed bar path removes the balance requirement, allowing you to load your quads and glutes much heavier than a free-weight lunge.

name: Landmine Row
instructions: Secure one end of a barbell into a landmine attachment and load the other end with plates.
Straddle the bar so you are facing away from the landmine base.
Hook a V-grip handle securely underneath the bar, right up against the loaded plates.
Hinge at your hips, keeping your back flat and your chest up.
Pull the V-grip handle straight up toward your stomach, squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Lower the weight back down to a full stretch.
tips: This acts perfectly as a T-Bar row but using a standard barbell. Keep your lower back tightly braced.

name: Deficit Push-Up
instructions: Place your hands on parallettes, a pair of hex dumbbells, or stacked weight plates to elevate them slightly off the floor.
Assume a standard push-up position with a rigid core.
Lower your body between the elevated platforms, allowing your chest to sink lower than your hands.
Feel the deep stretch across your chest, then powerfully press back up to the starting position.
tips: The extra range of motion deeply stretches the pectoral muscle fibers, leading to greater hypertrophy. Go slowly if you have limited shoulder mobility.

name: TRX Hamstring Curl
instructions: Lower the TRX handles so the foot cradles are roughly a foot off the floor.
Lie on your back and place your heels securely into the foot cradles.
Lift your hips off the floor, forming a straight line from your shoulders to your heels.
Keep your hips elevated and pull your heels toward your glutes by contracting your hamstrings.
Slowly extend your legs back out straight without letting your hips drop.
tips: This is a grueling hamstring isolation exercise that requires immense core stability to keep your hips bridged. Dig your heels hard into the straps.

name: Dumbbell Lying Leg Curl
instructions: Lie face down on a flat bench or a mat on the floor.
Have a partner place a dumbbell vertically between the arches of your feet, or carefully scoop it up yourself.
Squeeze your feet tightly together to secure the dumbbell.
Curl the dumbbell up toward your glutes, keeping your hips pressed flat against the bench/floor.
Slowly lower the weight back down without fully locking your knees.
tips: This is an excellent way to isolate the hamstrings at home or in a gym without a leg curl machine. Squeeze your core to avoid arching your lower back.

name: Dumbbell Standing Calf Raise
instructions: Stand tall holding a dumbbell in each hand, letting your arms hang down by your sides.
Place the balls of your feet on the edge of a sturdy step, weight plate, or block, letting your heels hang off.
Lower your heels down toward the floor to deeply stretch the calves.
Push up through the balls of your feet as high as possible, contracting the calves forcefully.
Lower back down under control.
tips: Do not bounce out of the bottom position; pause briefly at the deepest stretch to remove the Achilles tendon's elastic energy.

name: Partner Assisted Glute-Ham Raise
instructions: Kneel on a soft mat with an upright torso.
Have a partner press down firmly on the backs of your ankles to lock your legs into the floor.
Keep your body rigid from your knees to your head.
Slowly lower your torso forward as far as you can control using purely hamstring strength.
When you can no longer control the descent, catch yourself lightly on the floor with your hands.
Perform a light push-up while simultaneously pulling with your hamstrings to return to the kneeling position.
tips: This is the same mechanism as the Nordic curl. Try to rely less on your hands and more on your hamstrings as you get stronger over time.

name: Banded Glute Bridge
instructions: Place a loop resistance band around your legs, positioned just above your knees.
Lie on your back with your knees bent and feet flat on the floor, hip-width apart.
Push your knees outward constantly to keep tension on the band.
Drive through your heels to lift your hips toward the ceiling, squeezing your glutes.
Hold for a second at the top, ensuring your knees are still driving against the band, then lower down.
tips: The band forces the gluteus medius (outer glute) to fire constantly to prevent your knees from caving in, making this a spectacular glute activator.

name: Alternating Dumbbell Curl
instructions: Stand tall holding a dumbbell in each hand by your sides, palms facing your legs (neutral grip).
Keep your torso completely still.
Curl the right dumbbell up toward your shoulder, rotating your wrist (supinating) so your palm faces your shoulder at the top.
Squeeze the right bicep, then slowly lower the weight back to the starting position.
Repeat the exact same motion with your left arm, continuing to alternate sides.
tips: Alternating arms allows you to focus 100% on the mind-muscle connection for one arm at a time. Do not lean to the side to help lift the weight.

name: Decline Dumbbell Flyes
instructions: Secure your legs at the top of a decline bench and lie back with a dumbbell in each hand.
Press the dumbbells straight up over your lower chest, palms facing each other, with a slight bend in your elbows.
Slowly lower the weights out to your sides in a wide arc until you feel a deep stretch in your chest.
Squeeze your chest muscles to bring the dumbbells back up along the same arc.
Stop just before the dumbbells touch at the top to maintain tension.
tips: Maintain the slight bend in your elbows throughout the entire movement. Do not turn this into a pressing motion.

name: Cable Rear Delt Fly
instructions: Set two opposing cable pulleys to slightly above shoulder height.
Reach your left hand across your body to grab the right cable, and your right hand to grab the left cable (the cables will cross in front of you).
Stand tall, brace your core, and keep a soft bend in your elbows.
Pull the cables outward and backward, uncrossing your arms until your hands are out to your sides.
Squeeze your rear delts and slowly return to the crossed starting position.
tips: Keep your arms parallel to the floor. Focus on pulling from the back of the shoulder rather than squeezing the shoulder blades together.

name: Barbell Pullover
instructions: Lie perpendicular across a flat bench, with only your upper back supporting you and your feet flat on the floor (hips bridged up).
Hold a barbell straight up over your chest with an overhand, shoulder-width grip.
Keeping your arms relatively straight (slight elbow bend), slowly lower the bar in an arc behind your head.
Go as deep as your shoulder mobility comfortably allows to stretch the lats.
Pull the bar back up in the same arc until it is over your chest.
tips: Using an EZ-Curl bar can relieve wrist strain. Keep your core heavily braced and do not let your hips sink as the weight goes back.

name: Dumbbell Side Lunge
instructions: Stand tall with your feet together, holding a dumbbell in each hand by your sides.
Take a large step straight out to your right side.
Keep your left leg completely straight and plant your right foot, pushing your hips back and bending your right knee into a squat on that side.
Lower your hips until your right thigh is parallel to the floor. The dumbbells should frame your right knee.
Push forcefully off your right foot to return to the starting, standing position.
Repeat on the left side.
tips: Keep your chest up and ensure your working knee tracks directly over your toes, not caving inward.

name: Banded Squat
instructions: Place a resistance band loop just above your knees.
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointed slightly outward.
Actively push your knees out against the band to keep it taut.
Push your hips back and bend your knees to lower into a squat, keeping your chest up.
Drive through your heels to stand back up, ensuring your knees do not cave in at any point.
tips: The band forces your gluteus medius to fire continuously to prevent knee valgus (caving), making this an excellent corrective exercise or warmup.

name: Box Squat
instructions: Place a box behind you in a squat rack. Set the height so your thighs are parallel to the floor when sitting.
Unrack the barbell and step back until you feel the box against your calves.
Take a wider-than-normal stance. Push your hips back and squat down until you are fully seated on the box.
Pause for a split second, releasing the tension in your hips, but keeping your core tight.
Drive explosively through your feet, pushing your upper back into the bar, to stand up.
tips: Do not bounce off the box; come to a complete, controlled stop. This builds immense starting strength from a static position.

name: Rack Pulls
instructions: Set the safety pins in a power rack to just above or just below your knees.
Load a barbell on the pins. Stand close to the bar with a hip-width stance.
Hinge at the hips and grab the bar with a double-overhand or mixed grip.
Keep your back flat, chest up, and brace your core.
Drive your hips forward and pull the bar up the rest of the way until you are fully standing and locked out.
Lower the bar back to a dead stop on the pins.
tips: By removing the bottom half of the deadlift, you can overload the upper back and grip heavily. Do not bounce the bar off the pins.

name: Seal Row
instructions: Place a flat bench on top of blocks or use a specialized tall Seal Row bench. Place a barbell underneath it.
Lie face down on the bench with your legs straight and hanging freely (or resting lightly on the floor).
Reach down and grab the barbell with a shoulder-width grip.
Pull the barbell straight up until it hits the underside of the bench.
Squeeze your upper back, then lower the bar until your arms are fully extended.
tips: This is arguably the strictest back isolation exercise possible, completely removing momentum and lower back involvement.

name: TRX Triceps Extension
instructions: Grab the TRX handles and stand facing away from the anchor point.
Lean forward so your body is straight and your arms are fully extended out in front of you, roughly at eye level.
Keeping your elbows completely stationary and pointing forward, bend them so your body lowers and your hands come toward your forehead.
Press through the handles using your triceps to extend your arms back to the starting position.
tips: Keep your core intensely braced so your hips don't sag. The further back you walk your feet, the heavier the resistance becomes.

name: TRX Bicep Curl
instructions: Grab the TRX handles and stand facing the anchor point.
Lean back until there is tension on the straps, extending your arms straight in front of you with palms facing up (supinated).
Keep your body in a rigid plank line.
Keeping your elbows high and stationary, curl the handles toward your temples.
Slowly extend your arms back to the starting position, resisting gravity.
tips: Do not let your elbows drop down as you curl; they should remain pointed toward the anchor point throughout the movement.

name: Weighted Plank
instructions: Have a partner carefully place a weight plate flat on your middle/upper back, or slowly maneuver one onto your back yourself from a kneeling position.
Assume a standard forearm plank position: elbows directly under shoulders, legs straight, and feet hip-width apart.
Squeeze your glutes, brace your core, and keep your body in a perfectly straight line.
Hold for the desired time.
Carefully drop your knees to the floor and have the partner remove the plate.
tips: Do not let the plate rest on your lower back/lumbar spine; it should sit squarely on your upper back/shoulder blades.

name: Hollow Body Hold
instructions: Lie flat on your back on a mat with your legs squeezed together and your arms reaching straight overhead.
Press your lower back firmly into the floor.
Lift your legs a few inches off the ground while simultaneously lifting your head and shoulders off the ground.
Your body should form a shallow 'U' or 'hollow' shape.
Hold this position, keeping the core intensely tight and ensuring the lower back never lifts off the floor.
tips: This is a foundational gymnastics core hold. If your lower back pops off the floor, raise your legs higher until you can regain the core connection.

name: Dead Bug
instructions: Lie flat on your back with your arms pointing straight up toward the ceiling.
Bring your legs up so your hips and knees are bent at 90-degree angles (tabletop position).
Press your lower back hard into the floor.
Slowly extend your right arm straight back behind your head while simultaneously extending your left leg straight out until it hovers over the floor.
Return both to the starting position and repeat with the left arm and right leg.
tips: The entire purpose of this exercise is to maintain a flat lower back while your limbs move. Move very slowly for maximum benefit.

name: Bird-Dog
instructions: Start on all fours (tabletop position) with hands under shoulders and knees under hips.
Keep your back flat and your neck neutral.
Simultaneously extend your right arm straight forward and your left leg straight backward until both are parallel to the floor.
Squeeze your glute and upper back at full extension, ensuring your hips do not rotate.
Return to the starting position and switch sides (left arm, right leg).
tips: This is the reverse of a Dead Bug. Do not lift your leg so high that your lower back arches. Keep your hips square to the floor.

name: Glute Bridge March
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the floor, hip-width apart.
Drive through your heels to bridge your hips up until your body forms a straight line from knees to shoulders.
Keeping your hips bridged high and completely level, lift your right foot off the floor and drive your right knee toward your chest.
Place your right foot back down and immediately drive your left knee toward your chest.
Continue 'marching' back and forth.
tips: Your hips will want to drop toward the side of the lifted leg. Brace your core and squeeze your planted glute hard to prevent any tilting.

name: Machine Assisted Pull-Up
instructions: Select the desired counterweight on the machine (the *heavier* the weight you select, the *easier* the exercise will be).
Climb onto the machine and place your knees (or feet, depending on the machine) on the pad.
Grasp the handles above with a wide overhand grip.
Lower yourself down slowly until your arms are fully extended.
Pull your chest up toward the bar, driving your elbows down toward the floor.
Slowly lower back down with control.
tips: Do not let the weight stack slam at the top or bottom. This is the best way to build the specific muscle patterns needed for a freestanding pull-up.

name: Machine Assisted Dip
instructions: Select a counterweight to assist you.
Place your knees securely on the pad and grip the parallel bars firmly.
Start with your arms fully extended, supporting your weight.
Lower your body by bending your elbows until your shoulders dip just below your elbows.
Press forcefully back up to the starting position.
tips: Lean your torso slightly forward to target the chest more, or keep your torso perfectly upright to target the triceps.

name: Curtsy Lunge
instructions: Stand tall holding a dumbbell in each hand, or with your hands on your hips.
Shift your weight to your right leg.
Take a step backward and slightly to the side with your left leg, crossing it behind your right leg (like a curtsy).
Bend both knees to lower your hips until your right thigh is parallel to the ground.
Drive through your right heel to return to the starting position.
Alternate sides or complete all reps on one leg first.
tips: The crossing motion deeply engages the gluteus medius (side of the glutes). Keep your torso upright and your front foot perfectly flat.

name: Cable Pullover
instructions: Place a flat bench in front of a low cable pulley. Attach a straight bar or rope.
Lie face up on the bench with your head close to the pulley.
Reach back and grab the attachment with both hands. Keep a slight bend in your elbows.
Pull the weight over your head and in front of your chest until the bar is over your stomach.
Slowly let the cable pull your arms back overhead until you feel a deep stretch in your lats.
tips: This provides a massive, constant stretch on the lats compared to a dumbbell pullover, which loses tension at the top of the movement.

name: Seated Barbell Calf Raise
equipment: Weight Plates (or a block to elevate toes)
instructions: Sit on the end of a flat bench. Place a block or two small weight plates on the floor in front of you.
Rest the balls of your feet on the block, letting your heels drop down to the floor.
Place a heavily padded barbell across your lower thighs, just above the knees.
Drop your heels to stretch the calves.
Drive through your toes to raise the barbell as high as possible, flexing the calves.
Lower back down slowly.
tips: This is a great workaround if your gym lacks a seated calf raise machine. Hold the barbell firmly with your hands to keep it from rolling.

name: Cable Upright Row
instructions: Attach a straight bar to the lowest pulley on a cable machine.
Stand facing the machine, grab the bar with an overhand grip (slightly narrower than shoulder-width), and step back to create tension.
Keep the bar close to your body and pull it straight up toward your chin.
Lead with your elbows, ensuring they stay higher than your wrists throughout the movement.
Pause at the top (around upper-chest height), then slowly lower the bar back to the starting position.
tips: Using a cable provides constant tension and often feels much smoother on the shoulder and wrist joints compared to a free barbell.

name: Kneeling Cable Crunch
instructions: Attach a rope to a high pulley and kneel on a mat facing the machine (or facing away, depending on preference).
Grab the rope with both hands and pull it down so your hands are positioned next to your ears or forehead.
Lock your hips in place; do not sit back onto your heels during the movement.
Flex your spine forward, curling your torso and bringing your elbows toward your knees.
Squeeze your abs intensely, then slowly rise back to the starting position.
tips: The weight should be moved entirely by your abs curving your spine, not by your hip flexors or arms pulling.

name: Single-Leg Glute Bridge
instructions: Lie on your back with your knees bent and feet flat on the floor, hip-width apart.
Lift your right leg off the floor and extend it straight out.
Drive through your left heel and squeeze your left glute to lift your hips toward the ceiling.
Bridge up until your body forms a straight line from your left knee to your shoulders.
Lower your hips back to the floor with control and repeat before switching legs.
tips: Ensure your hips stay perfectly level; do not let the hip of the elevated leg sag toward the floor.

name: Tibialis Raise
instructions: Stand with your back flat against a wall and walk your feet out about 12 to 18 inches in front of you.
Keep your legs completely straight and your heels planted firmly on the ground.
Flex your feet, pulling your toes up toward your shins as high as you can.
Hold the contraction for a second, feeling the burn in the front of your lower legs.
Slowly lower your toes back to the floor.
tips: This is a crucial, often-ignored exercise for bulletproofing the knees and preventing shin splints. Move your feet further from the wall to increase difficulty.

name: Spanish Squat
instructions: Loop a thick resistance band securely around a squat rack or heavy anchor point.
Step inside the band so it wraps around the backs of both calves, just below the back of the knee.
Walk backward until the band is extremely taut.
Keep your torso completely upright and sit back into a squat, letting the band support your weight and keeping your shins perfectly vertical.
Drive through your heels to stand back up, pushing your knees back against the band.
tips: Because your shins remain vertical, the shear force on the knee joint is significantly reduced, making this an amazing quad builder for people with knee pain.

name: Incline Hammer Curl
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 45 to 60-degree incline.
Lie back holding a dumbbell in each hand, letting your arms hang straight down to the floor.
Keep your palms facing each other (neutral grip).
Keeping your upper arms perfectly stationary, curl the dumbbells up toward your shoulders.
Squeeze your forearms and biceps at the top, then slowly lower the weights back to a dead hang.
tips: The incline stretches the long head of the bicep, while the neutral grip heavily targets the brachialis and forearm muscles.

name: Tate Press
instructions: Lie flat on a bench holding two dumbbells directly over your chest with your palms facing forward.
Keep the dumbbells touching each other.
Without moving your shoulders, bend your elbows outward to lower the dumbbells down to the center of your chest.
The dumbbells should trace an 'L' shape and rest lightly on your chest.
Press the weights forcefully back up, squeezing the triceps until your arms are locked out.
tips: This isolates the triceps powerfully while minimizing stress on the elbow joints compared to traditional skullcrushers.

name: JM Press
instructions: Lie on a flat bench and unrack a barbell using a close grip (about shoulder-width or slightly narrower).
Position the bar over your upper chest/throat.
Lower the bar by bending your elbows and allowing them to track forward toward your knees, not out to the sides.
The bar should come straight down toward your upper chest or neck.
Press the bar forcefully back up to the starting position.
tips: This is a hybrid between a close-grip bench press and a skullcrusher. Start very light to master the unusual bar path and elbow tracking.

name: Spoto Press
instructions: Set up exactly as you would for a standard barbell bench press.
Unrack the bar and lower it with control toward your chest.
Instead of touching your chest, stop the bar exactly 1 to 2 inches above your chest and bring it to a complete dead stop.
Hold the bar perfectly still in the air for 1-2 seconds, maintaining full back and core tension.
Explosively press the bar back up to lockout.
tips: This builds massive bottom-end pressing power and forces you to stay completely tight at the most vulnerable part of the lift.

name: Reverse Grip Bench Press
instructions: Lie on a flat bench and grip the barbell with an underhand (supinated) grip, hands slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Unrack the bar and hold it over your lower chest.
Lower the bar slowly to your lower chest/upper stomach area, keeping your elbows tucked tightly to your sides.
Press the bar back up in a slight arc toward your face until your arms are fully extended.
tips: Always use a spotter or safety pins for this variation, as a slip grip can be dangerous. It surprisingly activates the upper chest more effectively than standard flat benching.

name: Floor Wipers
instructions: Lie flat on your back holding a loaded barbell straight up over your chest (like the lockout of a floor press).
Keep your legs completely straight and together.
Lift your legs off the floor and sweep them up toward the right weight plate of the barbell.
Lower them back down to a hover, then sweep them up toward the left weight plate.
Continue alternating sides while keeping your lower back pressed into the floor and the barbell steady.
tips: The barbell acts purely as an anchor and a target. Use light weight on the bar. This is an intense core and oblique exercise.

name: Dragon Flag
instructions: Lie face up on a flat bench. Reach behind your head and grip the edges of the bench firmly.
Swing your legs up so your entire lower body and torso lift off the bench, pointing your toes toward the ceiling. Only your upper back and shoulders should touch the bench.
Keep your body perfectly straight from your toes to your chest.
Slowly lower your entire body as one rigid unit until it is hovering just above the bench.
Pull yourself back up to the vertical position.
tips: This is a highly advanced core move popularized by Bruce Lee. Do not bend at the hips; the movement should happen purely at the shoulders/upper back.

name: Hanging Windshield Wipers
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with an overhand grip.
Engage your lats and core to lift your toes all the way up to the bar (a strict toes-to-bar position).
Keeping your legs straight and your toes near the bar, rotate your hips to drop your legs to the right side (to about 3 o'clock).
Using your core, sweep your legs across your body over to the left side (to about 9 o'clock).
Continue sweeping back and forth.
tips: This requires massive grip, lat, and core strength. Do not let your body violently swing back and forth; control the rotation.

name: Landmine Russian Twist
instructions: Secure one end of a barbell into a landmine attachment and load the other end.
Stand facing the landmine with a shoulder-width stance, holding the sleeve of the barbell with both hands directly in front of your face/upper chest.
Keep your arms relatively straight with a slight bend in the elbows.
Pivot your feet and rotate your torso to lower the barbell down to your right hip.
Explosively use your core to swing the barbell back up to the center and immediately down to your left hip.
tips: Power should come from the hips and core rotating, not just your arms swinging the weight. Keep your chest up.

name: Landmine Squat
instructions: Secure a barbell in a landmine attachment. Stand facing it and hold the loaded end with both hands at chest level, interlocking your fingers.
Stand with your feet slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Keeping your elbows tucked, chest up, and the weight against your chest, push your hips back and squat down.
Lower until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor.
Drive through your heels to stand back up, leaning slightly into the weight as it arcs upward.
tips: The arc of the landmine naturally guides you into a deep, upright squat, making this extremely back-friendly compared to a barbell back squat.

name: Dumbbell Step-Down
instructions: Stand on top of a box or bench, holding a dumbbell in each hand.
Shift your weight to your right foot. Let your left foot hang off the edge of the box.
Slowly bend your right knee and push your hips back to lower your left heel toward the floor.
Take 3 to 5 seconds to control the descent. Tap your left heel softly on the floor.
Drive through your right foot to stand back up on the box.
tips: This heavily targets the eccentric (lowering) strength of the quads and glutes. Do not push off the floor with the resting foot.

name: B-Stance Hip Thrust
instructions: Set up for a standard hip thrust with your upper back resting on a bench and a barbell/dumbbell across your hips.
Stagger your feet: keep your right foot flat on the floor, and slide your left foot slightly forward, resting only on your left heel or toes.
80% of your weight should be driven through your flat right foot.
Drive through your right heel to thrust your hips to the ceiling.
Squeeze the glutes at the top, then lower back down.
tips: This acts as a single-leg hip thrust but uses the staggered leg for balance, allowing you to lift significantly heavier weight.

name: Frog Pumps
instructions: Lie on your back with your knees bent and falling outward. Press the soles of your feet together (butterfly position).
Pull your heels close to your glutes.
Tuck your chin to your chest and brace your core.
Drive the outsides of your feet into the floor and squeeze your glutes to lift your hips into the air.
Perform high, rapid repetitions, focusing entirely on the glute squeeze at the top.
tips: This is a targeted glute activator. Keep your chin tucked to prevent your lower back from arching. You can place a dumbbell on your hips to increase resistance.

name: Sled Drag
instructions: Attach long straps to a loaded sled. Hold the straps in your hands or wrap them around your waist/shoulders.
Face away from the sled. Lean forward into the straps so your body is at an angle.
Take large, powerful strides forward, dragging the sled behind you.
Keep your chest up and drive through your toes as you walk or run.
tips: This is incredible for developing forward-driving power and conditioning. The lack of eccentric loading makes it easy to recover from.

name: Yoke Walk
instructions: Set the crossbar of a Yoke so it rests comfortably across your upper back/traps (like a back squat).
Position yourself under the bar, brace your core intensely, and stand up to lift the Yoke off the ground.
Grasp the uprights with your hands to stabilize the frame.
Take short, quick, measured steps forward.
Keep your torso perfectly upright and breathe shallowly into your chest.
Lower the Yoke back to the ground with control once the distance is covered.
tips: This is one of the heaviest systemic loads the human body can endure. Your core must be braced like a steel pillar the entire time.

name: Belt Squat
instructions: Strap the belt securely around your waist/hips and attach it to the machine's cable or lever.
Stand on the platform with a shoulder-width stance.
Push your hips back and squat down, keeping your chest up and back flat.
Lower yourself until your thighs are at least parallel to the platform.
Drive through your heels to stand back up.
tips: Because the weight hangs from your hips instead of sitting on your shoulders, this exercise completely removes spinal compression while still heavily loading the legs.

name: Reverse Hack Squat
instructions: Position yourself in the hack squat machine facing the pad (the opposite of the standard position).
Place your shoulders under the pads and grip the handles.
Place your feet on the platform, shoulder-width apart.
Unrack the weight and push your hips backward, squatting down until your thighs are parallel to the platform.
Push through your heels and squeeze your glutes forcefully to return to the starting position.
tips: Facing the machine changes the biomechanics, placing a massive stretch and load onto the glutes and hamstrings rather than the quads.

name: Pendulum Squat
instructions: Step into the machine, placing your back flat against the pad and your shoulders under the shoulder pads.
Place your feet on the angled platform.
Unrack the machine and squat down deeply; the machine will swing in a natural arc.
Lower yourself as deeply as your mobility allows, then push through your mid-foot to stand back up.
tips: The pendulum arc mimics the natural path of a free-weight squat but provides back support, making it an incredible, joint-friendly quad builder.

name: Chest-Supported T-Bar Row
instructions: Lie face down on the pad so your upper chest is supported.
Reach down and grab the handles with an overhand or neutral grip.
Pull the weight up toward your chest, driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Slowly lower the weight until your arms are fully extended, feeling a stretch in your upper back.
tips: The chest pad completely prevents you from using your lower back or momentum to cheat the weight up, forcing strict upper back isolation.

name: Cable Shrug
instructions: Set a cable pulley to the lowest position and attach a straight bar.
Stand facing the machine, grasp the bar with a shoulder-width overhand grip, and step back slightly to create tension.
Keep your arms straight and elevate your shoulders toward your ears.
Hold the peak contraction for a second, then lower the bar with control.
tips: The cable provides a slightly different angle of resistance compared to free weights, pulling slightly forward as well as down, which intensely activates the traps.

name: Barbell Wrist Curl
instructions: Sit on a flat bench and grasp a barbell with an underhand (supinated) grip, hands roughly shoulder-width apart.
Rest your forearms flat on your thighs so your wrists and hands hang freely over your kneecaps.
Allow the barbell to roll down to your fingertips, opening your hands slightly.
Curl your fingers back up, then flex your wrists upward as high as they can go.
Squeeze the forearms at the top, then slowly lower back down.
tips: Use a light weight to ensure a full range of motion. Do not let your forearms lift off your thighs during the movement.

name: Reverse Barbell Wrist Curl
instructions: Sit on a flat bench and grasp a barbell with an overhand (pronated) grip.
Rest your forearms flat on your thighs with your wrists hanging over your knees.
Let your wrists drop down as far as possible.
Extend your wrists upward as high as they can go, lifting the knuckles toward the ceiling.
Pause at the top contraction, then lower slowly.
tips: The extensors are typically much weaker than the flexors, so you will need to use a significantly lighter weight than standard wrist curls.

name: Wrist Roller
instructions: Stand holding a wrist roller with both hands out in front of you at shoulder height.
Begin rolling the device by twisting your wrists backward (extension) one hand at a time, winding the rope around the dowel to lift the weight.
Once the weight reaches the top, slowly unwind it by rolling the device forward (flexion) until the weight returns to the floor.
Repeat the process, starting the roll in the opposite direction.
tips: This is one of the most brutal forearm exercises in existence. Keep your arms locked straight out; do not let them drop as your shoulders fatigue.

name: Ring Row
instructions: Grab the rings and lean back, walking your feet forward until your body is at an angle.
Keep your body perfectly straight from head to heels.
Start with your arms fully extended and your palms facing down (pronated).
Pull your chest to the rings, driving your elbows back.
As you pull, rotate the rings so your palms face each other (neutral grip) at the top of the movement.
Slowly lower yourself back down, rotating your hands back to the starting position.
tips: The freedom of the rings allows your wrists and shoulders to move through their most natural, pain-free path of rotation.

name: Ring Push-Up
instructions: Adjust the rings so they hang just above the floor.
Grasp the rings and assume a push-up position, walking your feet back so your body is straight.
Keep your core tight and lower your chest between the rings by bending your elbows.
Let the rings rotate naturally as you descend.
Push forcefully back up to the starting position.
tips: The extreme instability requires massive chest and core engagement to keep your hands from flying outward. Turn the rings outward slightly at the top to intensify the chest contraction.

name: Ring Chest Fly
instructions: Adjust the rings to hang about waist to knee height (the higher they are, the easier the movement).
Grasp the rings and lean forward into a push-up position.
Keep a slight bend in your elbows and slowly let your arms open outward to your sides.
Lower your chest toward the floor until you feel a deep stretch in your pecs.
Squeeze your chest to bring the rings back together directly under your chest.
tips: This is a highly advanced movement that places immense strain on the chest and shoulder stabilizers. Start on your knees if necessary.

name: Standing Ab Wheel Rollout
instructions: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, bend at the waist, and place the ab wheel on the floor directly in front of your toes.
Brace your core intensely.
Roll the wheel straight forward, extending your body until your chest is just hovering above the ground and your arms are fully extended overhead.
Use your abs and lats to pull the wheel back, hinging at the hips until you return to the starting standing position.
tips: This is a master-level core exercise. If you cannot do this without your lower back sagging, you must stick to the kneeling variation until you build more strength.

name: Toes-to-Bar (T2B)
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with an overhand grip.
Engage your lats to pull your shoulders down slightly.
In one fluid motion, contract your abs and hip flexors to lift your legs straight up until your toes make physical contact with the pull-up bar between your hands.
Control the descent, lowering your legs back to a dead hang without excessively swinging.
tips: In CrossFit, a 'kipping' motion is often used, but for strict core hypertrophy, perform this movement slowly without using momentum to swing your legs up.

name: Pegboard Climb
instructions: Grip the pegs and insert them into the lowest holes on the board.
Hang freely, keeping your elbows bent at a 90-degree angle to hold tension.
Remove one peg, perform a one-arm pull-up lock-off, and reach up to place the peg into a higher hole.
Transfer your weight, remove the lower peg, and repeat the process to climb the board.
Carefully reverse the motion to climb back down.
tips: Keep your body close to the board. Do not simply jump down when you reach the top; climbing down builds incredible eccentric strength and control.

name: Sandbag Over Shoulder
instructions: Stand with the sandbag vertically between your feet.
Squat down and weave your arms tightly around the center of the bag.
Drive through your legs and pull the bag into your lap, resting it on your thighs while in a low squat.
Wrap your arms over the top of the bag, take a deep breath, and explosively extend your hips, pulling the bag forcefully up your body.
Guide the bag over one shoulder and let it drop to the floor behind you.
Turn around and repeat, throwing it over the opposite shoulder.
tips: This is a primal strongman movement. Ensure your lower back remains flat during the initial pull from the floor.

name: Slider Hamstring Curl
equipment: Sliders (or Towels on a smooth floor)
instructions: Lie on your back with your knees bent and place your heels on the sliders.
Bridge your hips up into the air, squeezing your glutes.
Keeping your hips elevated, slowly push your heels out until your legs are completely straight.
Dig your heels into the sliders and forcefully pull them back toward your glutes.
Do not let your hips drop to the floor during the movement.
tips: This demands massive concentric hamstring strength. If pulling back is too difficult, focus only on the slow eccentric slide out, drop your hips, reset, and repeat.

name: Wall Ball
instructions: Stand an arm's length away from the wall, holding a medicine ball at your chest with your elbows tucked.
Drop into a full front squat, keeping your chest up.
Explode upward, driving through your heels, and use the momentum to throw the ball at a target high on the wall.
Catch the ball on the rebound and smoothly transition straight back into the next squat.
tips: Fluidity is key. Use the power of your legs to propel the ball upward; do not turn it into a strict shoulder press.

name: Svend Press
instructions: Stand tall and pinch two lightweight plates together between your open palms directly in front of your chest.
Squeeze your chest and push the plates forcefully together.
Press the plates straight out in front of you until your arms are fully extended.
Maintain the inward pressure on the plates, then slowly bring them back to your chest.
tips: You cannot use heavy weight for this. The resistance comes entirely from you actively squeezing the plates together as hard as possible to create isometric chest tension.

name: GHD Back Extension
instructions: Position yourself face down on the GHD with your hips resting squarely on top of the main pad and your feet locked in the rollers.
Start with your torso parallel to the floor, forming a straight line with your legs.
Slowly lower your upper body toward the floor, bending at the waist until you are hanging upside down.
Squeeze your lower back and glutes to pull your torso back up to parallel.
Do not hyperextend your spine past the parallel point.
tips: Ensure the pad is adjusted so your hips can fold freely over the edge. Cross your arms over your chest or hold a weight plate to increase resistance.

name: Landmine Lumberjack Press
instructions: Secure a barbell in a landmine attachment and hold the loaded end with both hands directly in front of your chest/face.
Adopt a shoulder-width stance and brace your core.
Press the barbell straight up and slightly forward until your arms are fully extended.
Slowly lower the barbell back to the starting position.
tips: By using two hands, you can load this significantly heavier than a single-arm landmine press, building great anterior shoulder and upper chest thickness.

name: Kneeling Squat
instructions: Kneel on a soft pad or mat inside a Smith machine or under a free barbell.
Position the bar across your upper back/traps exactly as you would for a back squat.
Keeping your chest up and torso straight, push your hips back to sit on your calves.
Drive through your shins and squeeze your glutes forcefully to press your hips back forward.
Lock out at the top in a tall kneeling position.
tips: This exercise removes the quadriceps almost entirely, making it a pure glute isolation movement. Squeeze hard at the top without hyperextending your lower back.

name: Jefferson Squat
instructions: Place a barbell on the floor and straddle it so it is between your legs.
Turn your front foot to point straight ahead and your back foot out to a 90-degree angle.
Squat down and grasp the bar with one hand in front of your body and the other behind your body.
Keep your chest up and back flat.
Stand straight up, lifting the barbell between your legs.
Squat back down, tapping the plates to the floor before the next rep.
tips: This is a unique, old-school lift that challenges your core heavily due to the asymmetrical stance. Switch your grip and foot positioning on alternating sets.

name: Donkey Kicks
instructions: Start on all fours with your hands under your shoulders and knees under your hips.
Keep your back flat and your core braced to prevent your lower back from sagging.
Keeping your right knee bent at a 90-degree angle, lift your right leg straight up behind you.
Drive the sole of your foot toward the ceiling until your thigh is parallel to the floor.
Squeeze your glute at the top, then slowly lower your knee back to the mat.
tips: The movement should come entirely from your hip extending, not from your lower back arching. Use an ankle weight or resistance band to increase difficulty.

name: Fire Hydrants
instructions: Start on all fours on a mat with your hands under your shoulders and knees under your hips.
Keep your core tight and your back flat.
Keeping your right knee bent at 90 degrees, raise your right leg straight out to the side.
Lift as high as you can without rotating your torso or dropping your left hip.
Lower the knee back to the starting position with control.
tips: Imagine balancing a glass of water on your lower back. Your torso should remain completely still while only the hip moves.

name: Heel Touches (Penguins)
instructions: Lie on your back with your knees bent and feet flat on the floor, close to your glutes.
Keep your arms straight down by your sides.
Lift your head and shoulders slightly off the ground to engage your core.
Crunch down your right side to tap your right heel with your right hand.
Immediately crunch down your left side to tap your left heel with your left hand.
Continue alternating sides rapidly while keeping your shoulder blades off the floor.
tips: Focus on contracting the sides of your stomach (the obliques) to pull your torso laterally. Do not strain your neck.

name: Flutter Kicks
instructions: Lie flat on your back on a mat. Place your hands underneath your glutes for lower back support.
Keep your legs straight and lift them about 6 inches off the floor.
Press your lower back firmly into the ground.
Rapidly lift one leg slightly higher while lowering the other leg (without letting it touch the ground).
Continue alternating legs in a fast, scissor-like motion.
tips: If your lower back starts to arch off the floor, lift your legs higher up into the air until you can firmly press your back down again.

name: Scissor Kicks
instructions: Lie flat on your back with your hands under your glutes for support.
Lift both legs straight up about 6 to 12 inches off the floor.
Spread your legs out wide into a V-shape.
Bring them back together, crossing your right ankle over your left ankle.
Open them back out to the V-shape, then cross your left ankle over your right ankle.
Continue alternating the cross continuously.
tips: Like flutter kicks, the critical focus must be keeping the lower back glued to the floor at all times.

name: Plank Jacks
instructions: Start in a standard forearm plank position with your feet together.
Keep your core intensely tight and your hips perfectly level.
Jump both feet out wide (like a jumping jack).
Quickly jump both feet back together to the starting position.
Continue jumping in and out at a rapid, controlled pace.
tips: Do not let your hips bounce up into the air as you jump. Your upper body should remain completely stationary.

name: Reverse Crunch
instructions: Lie on your back with your hands by your sides or holding onto a heavy object behind your head for stability.
Bend your knees to 90 degrees and lift your feet off the floor.
Contract your abs to pull your knees toward your chest, simultaneously rolling your pelvis and lower back off the floor.
Squeeze your abs hard at the top.
Slowly unroll your spine and lower your hips back to the floor with control.
tips: Do not use momentum to swing your legs up. The movement must be initiated by the abdominal muscles pulling the pelvis upward.

name: Seated Dip Machine
instructions: Sit on the machine and adjust the seat so the handles are level with your mid-chest.
Secure yourself under the lap pad if the machine has one.
Grasp the handles and keep your elbows tucked relatively close to your sides.
Press the handles down forcefully until your arms are fully extended.
Slowly let the handles rise back to the starting position.
tips: This is a great alternative to bodyweight dips for those who struggle with shoulder pain or lack the strength for parallel bars.

name: Decline Dumbbell Triceps Extension
instructions: Secure your legs in a decline bench and lie back with a dumbbell in each hand.
Press the dumbbells straight up over your chest.
Keeping your upper arms perfectly stationary, bend your elbows to lower the dumbbells down beside your ears.
Squeeze your triceps to press the dumbbells back up to lockout.
tips: The decline angle increases the stretch on the triceps at the bottom of the movement while keeping constant tension at the top.

name: Cross-Body Hammer Curl
instructions: Stand tall holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip (palms facing your legs).
Keep your right arm completely still.
Curl the left dumbbell across your torso toward your right shoulder.
Squeeze your bicep and forearm at the top.
Slowly lower the weight back down, then repeat with the right arm curling toward the left shoulder.
tips: Curling across the body places a unique, heavy emphasis on the brachialis muscle (which sits underneath the bicep) to create thicker-looking arms.

name: Cable Concentration Curl
instructions: Attach a D-handle to a low cable pulley.
Stand (or sit on a bench) sideways to the machine and grab the handle.
Lean forward slightly and rest the back of your working upper arm against your inner thigh to anchor it.
Curl the handle up toward your shoulder, squeezing the bicep.
Slowly lower the handle, resisting the constant pull of the cable.
tips: The cable provides uniform tension, unlike a dumbbell which loses tension at the bottom of a concentration curl.

name: Lying Cable Curl
instructions: Attach a straight bar to the lowest setting on a cable pulley.
Lie flat on your back on a mat, with your feet resting against the base of the machine.
Grasp the bar with an underhand grip.
Keep your elbows locked to the floor by your sides.
Curl the bar toward your chest, squeezing your biceps hard.
Slowly extend your arms back to the starting position.
tips: Lying on the floor makes it physically impossible to use body English or momentum to swing the weight up.

name: Single-Arm Lat Pulldown
equipment: Lat Pulldown Machine or High Cable
instructions: Attach a D-handle to a high pulley. Sit down and secure your legs under the pad.
Grasp the handle with one hand using a neutral grip (palm facing in) or overhand grip.
Keep your torso upright and pull the handle down to your shoulder, driving your elbow toward your back pocket.
Squeeze your lat hard at the bottom, then slowly let the cable pull your arm back to a full stretch.
tips: Working unilaterally allows for a deeper stretch and a stronger contraction, as well as fixing muscle imbalances between sides.

name: Single-Arm Dumbbell Pullover
instructions: Lie perpendicular across a flat bench, resting only your upper back and shoulders on the pad. Plant your feet firmly on the floor.
Hold a single dumbbell in one hand directly over your chest. Use your free hand to support your working arm or hold onto the bench.
Keep a slight bend in your working elbow.
Slowly lower the dumbbell back and behind your head until you feel a deep stretch in the lat.
Pull the weight back up to the starting position.
tips: This requires massive core stabilization to prevent your body from twisting off the bench. Start very light.

name: Incline Dumbbell Front Raise
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 45-degree incline.
Lie back on the bench holding a dumbbell in each hand, letting your arms hang straight down to the floor.
Keep your palms facing down (pronated) or facing each other (neutral).
Keeping your arms relatively straight, raise the dumbbells up until they are above your chest or slightly higher.
Slowly lower the weights back to a dead hang.
tips: The incline removes the ability to swing your torso, making this a much stricter and more difficult variation of the standing front raise.

name: Leaning Cable Lateral Raise
instructions: Set a cable pulley to the lowest position. Stand next to the machine, holding the frame with your inside hand.
Grasp the D-handle with your outside hand.
Lean away from the machine so your body is at a steep angle, supporting your weight with your inside arm.
Keep a slight bend in your working arm and raise the cable out to the side until it reaches shoulder height.
Slowly lower the cable back to your side.
tips: Leaning shifts the resistance curve, placing maximum tension on the lateral deltoid right at the very top of the movement.

name: Reverse Pec Deck Fly
instructions: Adjust the machine's arms so they are pushed all the way back.
Sit facing the machine pad, resting your chest against it.
Grasp the horizontal handles with an overhand grip.
Keep your arms slightly bent and pull the handles backward in a wide arc.
Squeeze your rear delts hard at the end of the movement.
Slowly return the handles to the front, maintaining tension.
tips: Avoid completely squeezing your shoulder blades together; try to isolate the movement to just the backs of your shoulders to hit the rear delts perfectly.

name: Smith Machine Calf Raise
instructions: Place a step, block, or heavy weight plates directly under the bar of a Smith machine.
Step onto the block so just the balls of your feet are supported, and your heels hang off.
Position the bar across your upper back/traps and unrack it.
Lower your heels toward the floor for a deep stretch.
Push forcefully up onto your toes, flexing your calves as high as possible.
Lower back down with control.
tips: The Smith machine provides the balance that a free barbell lacks, allowing you to load the calves much heavier safely.

name: Reverse-Grip Triceps Pushdown
instructions: Attach a straight bar to a high cable pulley.
Grasp the bar with an underhand (supinated) grip, hands shoulder-width apart.
Stand tall, brace your core, and tuck your elbows firmly against your ribs.
Push the bar straight down toward your thighs until your arms are fully locked out.
Squeeze your triceps at the bottom, then slowly let the bar return to chest height.
tips: The underhand grip shifts the focus slightly to the medial head of the triceps. You won't be able to lift as much weight as a standard overhand pushdown.

name: Single-Arm Dumbbell Preacher Curl
instructions: Sit at a preacher curl bench with a dumbbell in one hand.
Rest the back of your working arm firmly against the angled pad, ensuring your armpit is snug over the top edge.
Let the dumbbell hang down until your arm is nearly fully extended.
Curl the dumbbell up toward your shoulder, focusing purely on the bicep contraction.
Lower it back down slowly.
tips: Working one arm at a time allows you to use your free hand for self-spotting forced reps if needed.

name: Behind-the-Back Barbell Wrist Curl
instructions: Stand facing away from a barbell resting on a rack at mid-thigh height, or have a partner hand it to you.
Grasp the barbell behind your back with an overhand grip (palms facing backward), hands shoulder-width apart.
Let the barbell roll down into your fingertips.
Curl your fingers up and flex your wrists forcefully, lifting the barbell as high as possible without bending your elbows.
Slowly lower back down to the fingertips.
tips: This is a classic mass-builder for the inner forearms that avoids the wrist strain some people feel with seated wrist curls.

name: Dumbbell Squeeze Press (Hex Press)
instructions: Lie flat on a bench with a dumbbell in each hand.
Press the dumbbells together directly over the center of your chest. The flat sides of the dumbbells (hex dumbbells work best) should be pressed hard against each other.
Squeeze your chest muscles intensely to keep the dumbbells pushed together.
Lower the dumbbells down to your sternum, maintaining the inward pressure.
Press the weights back up to lockout.
tips: The weight will be lighter than a standard bench press. The entire point is the isometric squeeze against the weights, forcing the inner chest to work constantly.

name: Plyometric Push-Up (Clapping Push-Up)
instructions: Start in a standard push-up position with a braced core.
Lower your chest toward the floor smoothly.
Explosively push off the floor with enough force to launch your hands entirely into the air.
Quickly clap your hands together under your chest before gravity pulls you back down.
Land softly on your hands, immediately absorbing the impact by bending your elbows into the next repetition.
tips: This builds fast-twitch muscle fibers and pressing power. Do not attempt until you can comfortably perform 20+ strict standard push-ups.

name: Archer Push-Up
instructions: Assume a push-up position but place your hands significantly wider than shoulder-width, with your fingers pointing outward.
Keeping your body straight, lower your chest entirely toward your right hand while extending your left arm completely straight out to the side.
Push forcefully off your right hand to return to the center.
Lower your chest toward your left hand, extending your right arm straight.
Alternate sides.
tips: This simulates a one-arm push-up by shifting almost all your body weight onto the bending arm. Keep your core tight to prevent your hips from rotating.

name: Zercher Lunge
instructions: Set a barbell in a rack at waist height. Hook your arms under the bar so it rests securely in the crooks of your elbows.
Clasp your hands together to secure the bar against your chest, and step back.
Stand tall, keeping your torso incredibly upright.
Take a large step backward with one foot and lower into a lunge until your back knee hovers over the floor.
Drive through your front heel to stand back up, keeping the barbell pulled tightly into your body.
tips: The front-loaded position of the Zercher grip forces your core and upper back to work overtime to keep you from collapsing forward.

name: Single-Leg Leg Press
instructions: Sit in the leg press machine and place only your right foot on the center of the sled.
Keep your left leg bent and out of the way, resting lightly on the floor or the frame.
Unrack the weight and slowly bend your right knee to lower the sled as deep as your mobility allows without your lower back rounding.
Drive through the heel of your right foot to push the sled back up.
Complete all reps, then switch to the left leg.
tips: This is excellent for fixing strength imbalances between legs while safely loading the quads without requiring balance.

name: Barbell Step-Up
instructions: Place a barbell across your upper traps, just as you would for a back squat.
Stand facing a sturdy box or bench.
Place your right foot completely flat onto the box.
Drive through your right foot to lift your entire body up onto the box until your right leg is straight.
Control your descent as you slowly step back down to the floor, leading with your left leg.
Alternate legs or complete one side at a time.
tips: Use a significantly lighter weight than your back squat. Avoid pushing off the floor with your trailing leg.

name: Dumbbell Clean
instructions: Stand tall with a dumbbell in each hand resting by your sides.
Hinge at the hips, bend your knees slightly, and lower the dumbbells just below your knees.
Explosively extend your hips, knees, and ankles (triple extension) while shrugging your shoulders.
Use the momentum to pull the dumbbells straight up, then quickly rotate your elbows underneath them to 'catch' the dumbbells at shoulder level.
Stand up straight, then lower the dumbbells back to your sides.
tips: Do not curl the weight. The arms merely guide the dumbbells upward; the power should come exclusively from your hips and legs.

name: Dumbbell Push Press
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand resting on your shoulders (palms facing forward or neutral).
Keep your chest up and perform a quick, shallow dip by bending your knees slightly.
Explosively drive your legs straight to propel the dumbbells upward.
Finish pressing the dumbbells straight overhead until your arms are fully locked out.
Lower the weights back to your shoulders with control.
tips: The leg drive allows you to use heavier dumbbells than a strict overhead press, making this a phenomenal shoulder mass builder.

name: Medicine Ball Russian Twist
instructions: Sit on a mat holding a medicine ball with both hands in front of your chest.
Lean back slightly to a 45-degree angle to engage your core, and lift your feet off the floor (or keep them planted for less difficulty).
Rotate your torso to the right, tapping the medicine ball lightly on the floor beside your hip.
Immediately rotate your torso all the way to the left, tapping the ball on the floor beside your left hip.
Continue twisting back and forth in a controlled manner.
tips: Ensure you are actually rotating your shoulders and torso, not just swinging your arms back and forth while looking straight ahead.

name: TRX Chest Fly
instructions: Grab the TRX handles and stand facing away from the anchor point.
