Lean forward into a suspended push-up position with your arms extended straight in front of you.
Keep a slight bend in your elbows and a perfectly rigid plank body position.
Slowly open your arms out to your sides, lowering your chest toward the floor.
When you feel a deep stretch in your chest, squeeze your pecs to pull the handles back together in front of you.
tips: This is incredibly difficult and requires high shoulder stability. Walk your feet forward (closer to the anchor point) to increase the angle and make it easier.

name: Gymnastic Ring Rollout
instructions: Adjust the rings to hang a few inches off the floor.
Kneel on the floor, grip the rings, and assume a kneeling push-up position.
Keep your core immensely tight, tuck your pelvis, and slowly extend your arms forward.
Let your hips and torso fall forward together as your arms extend out past your head.
Go as far as you can without breaking your straight lower back position, then pull the rings back to the starting position.
tips: The rings add multi-directional instability compared to an ab wheel, engaging the shoulder stabilizers even more heavily.

name: Reverse-Grip Lat Pulldown
instructions: Sit at a lat pulldown machine and secure your legs under the pad.
Grasp the bar with an underhand (supinated) grip, hands shoulder-width apart.
Lean back slightly, keep your chest high, and pull the bar down toward your lower chest/upper stomach.
Squeeze your lats and drive your elbows down and back.
Slowly let the weight back up until your arms are fully extended.
tips: The underhand grip shifts more focus to the lower lat fibers and heavily recruits the biceps. Do not swing your torso.

name: Dumbbell Upright Row
instructions: Stand tall holding a dumbbell in each hand resting against your thighs, palms facing your legs.
Keep the dumbbells close to your body and pull them straight up toward your collarbones.
Lead the movement with your elbows, ensuring they remain higher than your wrists at the top of the pull.
Pause and squeeze your shoulders, then slowly lower the weights.
tips: Dumbbells are often more comfortable than a barbell because they allow your wrists and shoulders to move freely, preventing impingement.

name: Standing Cable Oblique Crunch
instructions: Set a cable pulley to the highest position and attach a single D-handle.
Stand sideways to the machine, grasping the handle with the hand closest to the pulley.
Keep your arm completely straight and locked down by your side.
Crunch your torso straight down to the side, sliding your hand down your leg toward your knee.
Squeeze your oblique, then slowly return to an upright position.
Complete reps, then switch sides.
tips: Do not lean forward or backward; imagine you are trapped between two panes of glass. Let the weight stretch your oblique on the way up.

name: Double Kettlebell Clean
instructions: Stand over two kettlebells with a slightly wider-than-shoulder-width stance.
Hinge at the hips, grasp both handles, and hike them back between your legs.
Explosively drive your hips forward to propel the kettlebells upward.
Keep your elbows tucked into your ribs and quickly punch your hands up to 'catch' both kettlebells smoothly in the rack position (resting in the crook of your arms).
Let the kettlebells fall back down between your legs into the next repetition.
tips: This doubles the core and lower back demand compared to a single-arm clean. Keep your core tight and don't let the bells smack your forearms.

name: Barbell Muscle Snatch
instructions: Stand with the barbell resting against your shins, gripping it with a very wide (snatch) grip.
Keep your chest up and back flat. Explosively drive through your legs and extend your hips to pull the bar upward.
Keep the bar extremely close to your body.
Once the bar passes your chest, forcefully press it straight overhead until your arms lock out.
Unlike a full snatch, do *not* rebend your knees to drop under the bar. Your legs should be straight when the bar goes overhead.
tips: This removes the technical 'catch' phase of a snatch, heavily emphasizing raw upper back, shoulder, and pulling power.

name: Kettlebell Front Squat
instructions: Clean two kettlebells into the rack position (resting on the outsides of your forearms, elbows tucked tight to your ribs).
Set your feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointed slightly outward.
Keep your chest high, brace your core against the front-loaded weight, and squat down until your thighs are parallel to the floor.
Drive through your mid-foot to stand back up, keeping the kettlebells securely racked.
tips: The front-loaded weight profile is identical to a barbell front squat but taxes the upper back and core even harder to keep the torso upright.

name: Cable Iron Cross
instructions: Set two opposing cable pulleys to the highest position and attach D-handles.
Stand exactly in the middle of the station and grab a handle in each hand.
Keep a slight bend in your elbows and a proud chest.
Pull the handles straight down and inward to your sides until they touch your outer thighs.
Squeeze your lower chest hard at the bottom.
Slowly let your arms rise back up to the starting 'T' position.
tips: Unlike a standard cable crossover that pulls to the front of your torso, the Iron Cross pulls straight down to your sides, heavily emphasizing the lower and outer pec fibers.

name: Barbell Clean and Jerk
instructions: Stand over the barbell with a shoulder-width stance, grip the bar slightly wider than your legs, and drop your hips.
Explosively extend your hips and legs to pull the bar upward, shrugging your shoulders.
Quickly drop under the bar, rotating your elbows forward to catch the bar across your front deltoids (the Clean).
Stand up out of the squat.
Dip your knees slightly, then explosively drive the barbell straight overhead while splitting your legs forward and backward (the Jerk).
Bring your feet back together under the locked-out bar, then safely drop it to the floor.
tips: This is a highly technical Olympic lift. Break it down and master the Clean and the Push Jerk separately before combining them.

name: TRX Fallout
instructions: Grab the TRX handles and stand facing away from the anchor point.
Lean forward into a suspended push-up position, arms fully extended in front of you.
Brace your core tightly so your body forms a perfectly rigid plank.
Slowly raise your arms straight up overhead, allowing your body to fall forward toward the floor.
Go as far as you can without your lower back sagging.
Pull your hands back down to the starting position.
tips: This functions identically to an ab wheel rollout but from a standing position. Adjust your foot placement to change the difficulty (closer to the anchor is harder).

name: Sandbag Front Squat
instructions: Clean a heavy sandbag up to your chest.
Bear-hug the bag tightly against your torso, interlocking your fingers if possible.
Keep your chest tall, brace your core, and push your hips back to descend into a squat.
Lower yourself until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor.
Drive through your heels to stand back up, keeping the bag firmly clamped to your chest.
tips: The shifting sand forces your core to work incredibly hard to keep you upright. Take shallow breaths into your chest since the bag restricts belly breathing.

name: Medicine Ball V-Up
instructions: Lie flat on your back holding a medicine ball with both hands straight overhead on the floor.
Keep your legs perfectly straight.
In one explosive movement, lift your legs and your torso simultaneously, bringing the medicine ball to touch your toes.
Balance on your sit bones for a split second.
Slowly lower your arms and legs back down to the floor under control.
tips: Do not let the ball or your heels rest on the floor between reps to maintain constant tension on your abdominal wall.

name: Deficit Reverse Lunge
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand, with both feet planted on a low platform or weight plate (2-4 inches high).
Take a large step backward off the platform with your right foot.
Lower your hips straight down until your left thigh is parallel to the floor and your right knee hovers just above the ground.
Drive forcefully through your elevated left foot to step back up onto the platform.
Alternate legs or complete all reps on one side.
tips: The deficit significantly increases the range of motion for the front leg, placing an incredible stretch and load on the glutes and quads.

name: Handstand Hold
instructions: Place your hands on the floor about a foot away from a sturdy wall, shoulder-width apart.
Kick your legs up into the air until your heels rest lightly against the wall for balance.
Lock your arms out completely and shrug your shoulders up toward your ears.
Brace your core, squeeze your glutes, and keep your body in a perfectly straight line.
Hold this inverted position for the desired time, then safely kick one leg down at a time.
tips: Do not let your lower back arch wildly (the 'banana' back). Focus on pressing the floor away from you continuously.

name: Ring Face Pull
instructions: Grab the rings and lean back, walking your feet forward so your body is at an angle.
Start with your arms fully extended in front of you.
Pull the rings straight toward your face, letting your elbows flare out high and wide.
As you pull, rotate the rings so your knuckles end up facing your ears.
Squeeze your upper back intensely, then slowly lower yourself back to full extension.
tips: This is a fantastic bodyweight alternative to cable face pulls. Your bodyweight dictates the resistance, so walk backwards to make it easier.

name: Kettlebell Push Press
instructions: Clean two kettlebells into the rack position, resting securely on your forearms with elbows tucked tight to your chest.
Perform a rapid, shallow dip by bending your knees slightly.
Explosively drive your legs straight to launch the kettlebells upward.
Finish the movement by pressing your arms out until the bells are locked directly overhead.
Lower the kettlebells safely back into the rack position, absorbing the impact with your legs.
tips: The kettlebell rack position places the weight lower and closer to your center of gravity than dumbbells, requiring a slightly different pressing groove.

name: Suitcase Deadlift
instructions: Stand next to a heavy kettlebell or dumbbell positioned on the outside of your right foot.
Hinge at the hips and squat down to grip the weight with your right hand.
Keep your shoulders perfectly level and your torso perfectly straight (do not lean to the right to grab the weight).
Drive through the floor and push your hips forward to stand up, pulling the weight off the floor.
Lower the weight back to a dead stop on the floor, complete your reps, and switch sides.
tips: This is half deadlift, half core-stabilizer. Your body will naturally want to twist and lean toward the weighted side; your goal is to fight that completely.
